Fails\\n**Symptoms**: Cannot download all-MiniLM-L6-v2 from HuggingFace\\n\\n**Fallback A**: Use smaller/older model\\n```python\\n# Try alternative models in order of preference\\nmodel_names = [\\n    'all-MiniLM-L6-v2',  # Primary choice (fast, good quality)\\n    'paraphrase-MiniLM-L3-v2',  # Smaller, faster\\n    'all-mpnet-base-v2',  # Larger, better quality (slower)\\n]\\nfor name in model_names:\\n    try:\\n        model = SentenceTransformer(name)\\n        break\\n    except:\\n        print(f'Failed to load {name}, trying next...')\\n```\\n\\n**Fallback B**: Use simple TF-IDF cosine similarity instead of SBERT\\n```python\\nfrom sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer\\nfrom sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity\\n\\ndef compute_tfidf_similarity(sentences):\\n    vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer()\\n    tfidf = vectorizer.fit_transform(sentences)\\n    similarity_matrix = cosine_similarity(tfidf)\\n    return similarity_matrix\\n# Use this instead of SBERT embeddings\\n```\\n\\n### Issue 3: Graph Too Large / Computationally Expensive\\n**Symptoms**: O(n^2) edges for semantic graph, too slow for long texts\\n\\n**Fallback**: Use k-nearest-neighbors graph instead of threshold-based\\n```python\\ndef build_knn_graph(sentences, embeddings, k=5):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Connect each sentence to its k most similar neighbors.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    n = len(sentences)\\n    G = nx.Graph()\\n    \\n    for i in range(n):\\n        G.add_node(i)\\n    \\n    for i in range(n):\\n        # Compute similarities to all other sentences\\n        sims = [np.dot(embeddings[i], embeddings[j]) for j in range(n) if j != i]\\n        # Get indices of k most similar\\n        top_k_indices = np.argsort(sims)[-k:]\\n        for j in top_k_indices:\\n            if j > i:  # Avoid duplicates\\n                sim = sims[j] if j < i else sims[j-1]\\n                G.add_edge(i, j, weight=1.0/(1.0+sim))\\n    \\n    return G\\n```\\n\\n### Issue 4: Kirchhoff Index Computation Fails (Numerical Issues)\\n**Symptoms**: Laplacian pseudoinverse fails, singular matrix errors\\n\\n**Fallback A**: Use NetworkX resistance_distance for pairwise distances\\n```python\\n# Compute average resistance distance instead of full Kirchhoff index\\ndef compute_avg_resistance_distance(G):\\n    n = G.number_of_nodes()\\n    total = 0\\n    count = 0\\n    for i in range(n):\\n        for j in range(i+1, n):\\n            try:\\n                rd = nx.resistance_distance(G, i, j)\\n                total += rd\\n                count += 1\\n            except:\\n                pass\\n    return total / count if count > 0 else 0\\n```\\n\\n**Fallback B**: Use graph diameter or average shortest path as proxy\\n```python\\ndef compute_graph_connectivity_proxy(G):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Use average shortest path length as readability proxy.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if nx.is_connected(G):\\n        avg_path = nx.average_shortest_path_length(G)\\n    else:\\n        # For disconnected graphs, use diameter of largest component\\n        largest_cc = max(nx.connected_components(G), key=len)\\n        subgraph = G.subgraph(largest_cc)\\n        avg_path = nx.average_shortest_path_length(subgraph)\\n    return avg_path\\n```\\n\\n### Issue 5: Too Few Distinct Values (<10)\\n**Symptoms**: Metric produces nearly constant values across diverse texts\\n\\n**Cause**: Normalization scheme or graph construction is wrong\\n\\n**Fix**: Try different approaches\\n```python\\n# 1. Try different similarity thresholds\\nfor threshold in [0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7]:\\n    # Test which produces most distinct values\\n    pass\\n\\n# 2. Try different edge weighting schemes\\n# weight = 1 - cosine (distance)\\n# weight = exp(-cosine) (exponential)\\n# weight = 1 / (1 + exp(-cosine)) (sigmoid)\\n\\n# 3. Try RAW Kirchhoff index without normalization\\n# The raw Kf already varies with text structure\\n```\\n\\n### Issue 6: Partial Correlation Not Significant\\n**Symptoms**: Resistance metric correlates with sentence count, not unique variance\\n\\n**Interpretation**: Metric is measuring length, not discourse coherence\\n\\n**Fix**: Redesign graph to isolate discourse structure\\n```python\\n# Option A: Use ONLY semantic edges (no sequential edges)\\n# This ensures metric captures semantic connectivity, not just sequence\\n\\ndef build_pure_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, threshold=0.3):\\n    G = nx.Graph()\\n    for i in range(len(sentences)):\\n        G.add_node(i)\\n    # ONLY add semantic edges, no sequential edges\\n    # ... add edges based on similarity ...\\n    return G\\n\\n# Option B: Residualize sentence count from both variables before correlating\\n# (Already implemented in main plan, but can also do as preprocessing step)\\n```\\n\\n### Issue 7: Correlation with Human Judgments is Low (r < 0.3)\\n**Symptoms**: Metric doesn't predict readability\\n\\n**Interpretation**: Hypothesis may be wrong, or implementation has bugs\\n\\n**Fallback Experiment**: Check if traditional formulas work on this dataset\\n```python\\n# Validate that the CLEAR corpus can be predicted at all\\nimport textstat\\n\\nflesch_scores = [textstat.flesch_reading_ease(t) for t in texts[:100]]\\nr, p = stats.pearsonr(flesch_scores, human_scores[:100])\\nprint(f'Flesch Reading Ease correlation: r={r:.4f}')\\n\\nif r < 0.3:\\n    print('WARNING: Even traditional formulas fail on this dataset.')\\n    print('Dataset may not be suitable for readability assessment.')\\n```\\n\\nIf all baselines fail, document this and switch to a different dataset (WeeBit or Newsela).\\n\\n### Issue 8: Runtime Exceeds 6h Limit\\n**Symptoms**: Processing 5,000 texts takes too long\\n\\n**Fallback**: Subsample and use efficient approximations\\n```python\\n# 1. Subsample to 500-1000 texts for main experiment\\ntexts = texts[:500]\\nhuman_scores = human_scores[:500]\\n\\n# 2. Use multiprocessing\\nfrom multiprocessing import Pool\\nimport os\\n\\ndef process_text(text):\\n    kf, n, G = compute_resistance_metric(text)\\n    return kf, n\\n\\nwith Pool(os.cpu_count()) as pool:\\n    results = pool.map(process_text, texts)\\n\\n# 3. Cache SBERT embeddings\\nimport pickle\\n\\ndef cached_embeddings(texts, cache_file='embeddings_cache.pkl'):\\n    try:\\n        with open(cache_file, 'rb') as f:\\n            return pickle.load(f)\\n    except:\\n        embeddings = model.encode(texts)\\n        with open(cache_file, 'wb') as f:\\n            pickle.dump(embeddings, f)\\n        return embeddings\\n```\",\n  \"testing_plan\": \"## TESTING PLAN - Validate Before Full Execution\\n\\n### Phase T1: Unit Tests for Graph Construction\\n```python\\ndef test_graph_construction():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Test that semantic graph construction works correctly.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    # Test 1: Simple case - 2 similar sentences\\n    sentences = [\\n        \\\"The cat sat on the mat.\\\",\\n        \\\"A cat was sitting on a mat.\\\"\\n    ]\\n    embeddings = model.encode(sentences)\\n    G = build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, threshold=0.3)\\n    \\n    assert G.number_of_nodes() == 2\\n    assert G.number_of_edges() >= 1  # Should have at least 1 edge (similar sentences)\\n    print('Test 1 PASSED: Similar sentences connected')\\n    \\n    # Test 2: Dissimilar sentences - should have no edges (or fewer edges)\\n    sentences = [\\n        \\\"The cat sat on the mat.\\\",\\n        \\\"Quantum entanglement violates local realism.\\\"\\n    ]\\n    embeddings = model.encode(sentences)\\n    G = build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, threshold=0.5)  # Higher threshold\\n    \\n    # With threshold=0.5, dissimilar sentences may not get an edge\\n    print(f'Test 2: Dissimilar sentences, edges={G.number_of_edges()} (expect 0 or 1)')\\n    \\n    # Test 3: Isolated sentences (single sentence)\\n    sentences = [\\\"Single sentence.\\\"]\\n    embeddings = model.encode(sentences)\\n    G = build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, threshold=0.3)\\n    \\n    assert G.number_of_nodes() == 1\\n    assert G.number_of_edges() == 0\\n    print('Test 3 PASSED: Single sentence handled')\\n    \\n    # Test 4: Empty text\\n    sentences = []\\n    G = build_semantic_graph(sentences, np.array([]), threshold=0.3)\\n    assert G.number_of_nodes() == 0\\n    print('Test 4 PASSED: Empty text handled')\\n\\ndef test_sequential_vs_semantic():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Verify that semantic and sequential graphs are different.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    text = \\\"Alice went to the store. She bought some milk. The store was closed. It was a holiday.\\\"\\n    sentences = segment_sentences(text)\\n    embeddings = model.encode(sentences)\\n    \\n    G_sem = build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, threshold=0.3)\\n    G_seq = build_sequential_graph(sentences)\\n    \\n    print(f'Semantic graph edges: {G_sem.number_of_edges()}')\\n    print(f'Sequential graph edges: {G_seq.number_of_edges()}')\\n    \\n    assert G_sem.number_of_edges() != G_seq.number_of_edges(), \\\\\\n        'Semantic and sequential graphs should be different!'\\n    print('Test PASSED: Graphs are different')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T2: Test Resistance Computation\\n```python\\ndef test_resistance_computation():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Test Kirchhoff index computation on known graphs.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    # Test 1: Complete graph (all nodes connected)\\n    # For complete graph with n nodes and uniform edge weights:\\n    # Kirchhoff index = n - 1 (theoretical result)\\n    n = 5\\n    G_complete = nx.complete_graph(n)\\n    # Set edge weights to 1.0 (uniform resistance)\\n    for u, v in G_complete.edges():\\n        G_complete[u][v]['weight'] = 1.0\\n    \\n    kf = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G_complete)\\n    expected = n - 1  # Approximate for complete graph with unit weights\\n    print(f'Complete graph (n={n}): Kf={kf:.2f}, expected~{expected}')\\n    \\n    # Test 2: Path graph (sequential chain)\\n    G_path = nx.path_graph(5)  # 0-1-2-3-4\\n    for u, v in G_path.edges():\\n        G_path[u][v]['weight'] = 1.0\\n    \\n    kf_path = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G_path)\\n    print(f'Path graph (n=5): Kf={kf_path:.2f}')\\n    # Path graph should have higher resistance than complete graph\\n    assert kf_path > kf, 'Path graph should have higher resistance than complete graph'\\n    print('Test PASSED: Path graph has higher resistance than complete graph')\\n    \\n    # Test 3: Isolated nodes (disconnected graph)\\n    G_disconnected = nx.Graph()\\n    G_disconnected.add_nodes_from([0, 1, 2])\\n    G_disconnected.add_edge(0, 1, weight=1.0)\\n    # Node 2 is isolated\\n    \\n    try:\\n        kf_disc = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G_disconnected)\\n        print(f'Disconnected graph: Kf={kf_disc}')\\n        print('Warning: Disconnected graph should be handled specially')\\n    except Exception as e:\\n        print(f'Disconnected graph error (expected): {e}')\\n        print('Test PASSED: Disconnected graph detected')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T3: Test on Tiny Dataset (N=10)\\n```python\\ndef test_tiny_experiment():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Run experiment on 10 texts to verify pipeline works.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    # Create 10 synthetic texts with known readability\\n    test_texts = [\\n        # Easy texts (short sentences, simple words)\\n        \\\"The cat sat. The dog ran. They played.\\\",  # 3 short sentences\\n        \\\"I like apples. You like bananas. We all like fruit.\\\",  # 3 simple sentences\\n        # Medium texts\\n        \\\"The researcher conducted a comprehensive analysis of the data. Results indicated significant findings. The study was published in a journal.\\\",  # 3 medium sentences\\n        # Hard texts (long sentences, complex words)\\n        \\\"The epistemological underpinnings of contemporary hermeneutic phenomenology necessitate a nuanced understanding of ontological differentiation that transcends traditional metaphysical binaries.\\\",  # 1 very complex sentence\\n        \\\"Quantum entanglement, a phenomenon wherein particles become inextricably linked such that the state of one instantaneously influences the state of another regardless of spatial separation, challenges classical intuitions about locality and causality.\\\",  # 1 very long, complex sentence\\n    ] * 2  # Duplicate to get 10 texts\\n    \\n    # Synthetic readability scores (1-100)\\n    test_scores = [85, 80, 60, 30, 20, 85, 80, 60, 30, 20]\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TINY EXPERIMENT (N=10) ===')\\n    \\n    # Run evaluation\\n    results, valid_indices = evaluate_readability_metric(test_texts, test_scores)\\n    \\n    # Check results\\n    for scheme, res in results.items():\\n        print(f'\\\\nScheme: {scheme}')\\n        print(f'  Distinct values: {res[\\\"n_distinct_values\\\"]}')\\n        print(f'  Pearson r: {res[\\\"pearson_r\\\"]:.4f}')\\n        \\n        if res['n_distinct_values'] < 3:\\n            print('  WARNING: Too few distinct values even on tiny test!')\\n            print('  DIAGNOSTIC: Check graph construction and normalization')\\n    \\n    print('\\\\nTiny experiment complete. If results look reasonable, proceed to full experiment.')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T4: Validate CLEAR Corpus Loading\\n```python\\ndef test_clear_corpus_loading():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Test that CLEAR corpus can be downloaded and parsed.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TEST: CLEAR Corpus Loading ===')\\n    \\n    try:\\n        url = 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/scrosseye/CLEAR-Corpus/main/data/clear_corpus.csv'\\n        df = pd.read_csv(url)\\n        \\n        print(f'SUCCESS: Loaded {len(df)} rows')\\n        print(f'Columns: {df.columns.tolist()}')\\n        print(f'First 3 rows:')\\n        print(df.head(3))\\n        \\n        # Check for required columns\\n        text_col = None\\n        score_col = None\\n        \\n        for col in df.columns:\\n            if col.lower() in ['excerpt', 'text', 'passage']:\\n                text_col = col\\n            if 'readability' in col.lower() or 'score' in col.lower():\\n                score_col = col\\n        \\n        if text_col and score_col:\\n            print(f'SUCCESS: Found text column \\\"{text_col}\\\" and score column \\\"{score_col}\\\"')\\n            print(f'Score range: [{df[score_col].min()}, {df[score_col].max()}]')\\n            \\n            # Check a sample text\\n            sample_text = df[text_col].iloc[0]\\n            print(f'Sample text (first 100 chars): {sample_text[:100]}')\\n            \\n            return True\\n        else:\\n            print('WARNING: Could not identify text/score columns')\\n            print('Executor must manually inspect and specify column names')\\n            return False\\n            \\n    except Exception as e:\\n        print(f'FAILED: {e}')\\n        print('Will use fallback dataset (agentlans/readability)')\\n        return False\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T5: Runtime Test\\n```python\\ndef test_runtime_scaling():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Verify that runtime is acceptable for typical text lengths.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TEST: Runtime Scaling ===')\\n    \\n    test_cases = [\\n        ('Short (5 sentences)', 'This is a test. It has few sentences. Not very long. But it works. The end.'),\\n        ('Medium (10 sentences)', 'Sentence one. Sentence two. Sentence three. Sentence four. Sentence five. Sentence six. Sentence seven. Sentence eight. Sentence nine. Sentence ten.'),\\n        ('Long (20 sentences)', 'S1. ' * 20),  # Will be processed as 20 sentences\\n    ]\\n    \\n    for name, text in test_cases:\\n        start = time.time()\\n        kf, n, G = compute_resistance_metric(text, similarity_threshold=0.3)\\n        elapsed = time.time() - start\\n        \\n        print(f'{name}: n={n}, time={elapsed:.3f}s, Kf={kf:.2f}')\\n        \\n        if elapsed > 5.0:\\n            print(f'  WARNING: Slow! {elapsed:.1f}s for {n} sentences')\\n            print(f'  SUGGESTION: Use k-NN graph or increase similarity threshold')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T6: Validate Distinct Values Requirement\\n```python\\ndef test_distinct_values():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"CRITICAL TEST: Verify metric produces enough distinct values.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TEST: Distinct Values (Reviewer Issue #1) ===')\\n    \\n    # Load a few diverse texts from CLEAR corpus (or use test texts)\\n    test_texts = [\\n        \\\"Short simple text. Few sentences.\\\",  # Very short\\n        \\\"This is a medium length text. It has a few more sentences. Each sentence is moderately complex. The ideas are somewhat connected.\\\",  # Medium\\n        \\\"The epistemological ramifications of quantum decoherence necessitate a paradigmatic reconceptualization of macroscopic realism that transcends classical metaphysical frameworks.\\\" * 5,  # Very complex, repeated\\n        \\\"Alice went to the store. Bob went to the park. They met at the cafe. The cafe was closed. They went home.\\\" * 3,  # Narratives\\n    ]\\n    \\n    for scheme in ['raw', 'by_n', 'by_n_squared']:\\n        scores = []\\n        for text in test_texts:\\n            kf, n, G = compute_resistance_metric(text, threshold=0.3)\\n            if kf:\\n                normalized = normalize_kirchhoff_index(kf, n, scheme)\\n                scores.append(normalized)\\n        \\n        n_distinct = len(set([round(s, 4) for s in scores]))\\n        print(f'Scheme \\\"{scheme}\\\": {n_distinct} distinct values from {len(scores)} texts')\\n        \\n        if n_distinct <= 2:\\n            print('  CRITICAL FAILURE: Metric produces too few distinct values!')\\n            print('  This was the problem with the first experiment.')\\n            print('  DEBUG: Check graph construction - are all graphs identical?')\\n            print('  DEBUG: Check normalization - is division by n^2 collapsing values?')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T7: Integration Test (Full Pipeline on 50 Texts)\\n```python\\ndef integration_test():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Run full pipeline on 50 texts to catch integration issues.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== INTEGRATION TEST (50 texts) ===')\\n    \\n    # Try to load CLEAR corpus\\n    if test_clear_corpus_loading():\\n        # Use actual CLEAR data\\n        df = pd.read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/scrosseye/CLEAR-Corpus/main/data/clear_corpus.csv')\\n        texts = df['excerpt'].tolist()[:50]\\n        scores = df['readability_score'].tolist()[:50]\\n    else:\\n        # Use synthetic test data\\n        texts = [f'Test text number {i}. With some sentences. And ideas.' for i in range(50)]\\n        scores = list(range(50))  # Synthetic scores 0-49\\n    \\n    # Run full evaluation\\n    results, valid_idx = evaluate_readability_metric(texts, scores)\\n    \\n    # Check all reviewer issues\\n    print('\\\\n--- REVIEWER ISSUES CHECKLIST ---')\\n    \\n    for scheme, res in results.items():\\n        print(f'\\\\nScheme: {scheme}')\\n        \\n        # Issue 1: Continuous values\\n        print(f'  [1] Distinct values: {res[\\\"n_distinct_values\\\"]} (need >10: {\\\"PASS\\\" if res[\\\"n_distinct_values\\\"] > 10 else \\\"FAIL\\\"})')\\n        \\n        # Issue 2: Partial correlation\\n        print(f'  [2] Partial r (control sent count): {res[\\\"partial_r\\\"]:.4f} (need >0: {\\\"PASS\\\" if res[\\\"partial_r\\\"] > 0 else \\\"FAIL\\\"})')\\n        \\n        # Issue 3: Semantic edges\\n        print(f'  [3] Using semantic edges: True (by design)')\\n        \\n        # Issue 4: Established benchmark\\n        print(f'  [4] Using CLEAR corpus: True')\\n    \\n    print('\\\\nIntegration test complete. Review results before running full experiment.')\\n```\\n\\n### Test Execution Order\\n```\\n1. test_graph_construction()          # Unit test: graph building works\\n2. test_resistance_computation()     # Unit test: Kf computation works  \\n3. test_clear_corpus_loading()       # Integration: can load evaluation data\\n4. test_distinct_values()            # CRITICAL: metric isn't trivial\\n5. test_tiny_experiment()            # Integration: full pipeline on N=10\\n6. test_runtime_scaling()            # Performance: runtime acceptable?\\n7. integration_test()                # Full: 50 texts, all metrics\\n\\nOnly proceed to full experiment if ALL tests pass.\\n```\"\n}\n", "kind": "FileEditorObservation"}

'excerpt' or 'text': the text passage\\n# - 'readability_score' or 'score': human readability rating (1-100 scale)\\n# If column names differ, executor must map them\\n\\n# Map to standard names\\n# Common CLEAR format: text = excerpt, label = readability score (0-1 or 1-100)\\n# Executor: inspect actual columns and adapt\\ntext_col = 'excerpt'  # or 'text' - check actual\\nlabel_col = 'readability_score'  # or 'score' - check actual\\n\\ntexts = df[text_col].tolist()\\nscores = df[label_col].tolist()  # Human judgments on 1-100 scale\\n\\n# If scores are 0-1, convert to 1-100\\nif max(scores) <= 1.0:\\n    scores = [s * 100 for s in scores]\\n```\\n\\n### Step 1.2: Also Load Dependency Dataset (agentlans/readability)\\n```python\\nimport json\\n\\n# Load the agentlans readability dataset from dependency artifact\\n# This provides additional evaluation data with continuous scores\\nwith open('../../iter_1/gen_art/gen_art_dataset_1/full_data_out/full_data_out_1.json') as f:\\n    agentlans_data = json.load(f)\\n\\n# Use as secondary evaluation set\\n# Has 'input' (text) and 'output' (grade score 0-20)\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 2: GRAPH CONSTRUCTION WITH SBERT SEMANTIC EDGES\\n\\n### Step 2.1: Sentence Segmentation\\n```python\\nimport nltk\\nfrom nltk.tokenize import sent_tokenize\\n\\n# Ensure punkt available\\ntry:\\n    nltk.data.find('tokenizers/punkt')\\nexcept LookupError:\\n    nltk.download('punkt')\\n    nltk.download('punkt_tab')\\n\\ndef segment_sentences(text):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Split text into sentences. Returns list of sentence strings.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    sentences = sent_tokenize(text)\\n    # Filter out very short sentences (likely noise)\\n    sentences = [s.strip() for s in sentences if len(s.strip()) > 10]\\n    return sentences\\n```\\n\\n### Step 2.2: SBERT Embeddings\\n```python\\nfrom sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer\\nimport numpy as np\\n\\n# Load SBERT model (small, fast, good quality)\\nmodel = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')  # 384-dim embeddings\\n\\ndef get_sentence_embeddings(sentences):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Get SBERT embeddings for a list of sentences.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    embeddings = model.encode(sentences, show_progress_bar=False)\\n    return embeddings  # Shape: (n_sentences, 384)\\n```\\n\\n### Step 2.3: Build Discourse Graph with Semantic Edges\\n```python\\nimport networkx as nx\\n\\ndef build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, similarity_threshold=0.3, weight_scheme='inverse_cosine'):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Build discourse graph with SEMANTIC edges based on SBERT cosine similarity.\\n    \\n    Args:\\n        sentences: List of sentence strings\\n        embeddings: SBERT embeddings (n_sentences, 384)\\n        similarity_threshold: Minimum cosine similarity to add edge (try 0.3, 0.5)\\n        weight_scheme: How to convert similarity to resistance\\n            - 'inverse_cosine': weight = 1/(1+cosine) [resistance]\\n            - 'distance': weight = 1 - cosine [resistance distance]\\n            - 'similarity': weight = cosine [conductance]\\n    \\n    Returns:\\n        G: NetworkX graph with semantic edges\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    n = len(sentences)\\n    G = nx.Graph()\\n    \\n    # Add nodes (sentences)\\n    for i, sent in enumerate(sentences):\\n        G.add_node(i, sentence=sent[:50])  # Store first 50 chars as label\\n    \\n    # Compute cosine similarity matrix\\n    # embeddings is (n, 384), normalize to unit vectors\\n    norms = np.linalg.norm(embeddings, axis=1, keepdims=True)\\n    normalized_embeddings = embeddings / (norms + 1e-8)\\n    similarity_matrix = np.dot(normalized_embeddings, normalized_embeddings.T)  # (n, n)\\n    \\n    # Add edges where similarity > threshold\\n    edges_added = 0\\n    for i in range(n):\\n        for j in range(i+1, n):\\n            sim = similarity_matrix[i, j]\\n            if sim > similarity_threshold:\\n                # Convert similarity to resistance (weight = resistance)\\n                if weight_scheme == 'inverse_cosine':\\n                    # Resistance = 1/(1+cosine): high cosine -> low resistance\\n                    resistance = 1.0 / (1.0 + sim)\\n                elif weight_scheme == 'distance':\\n                    # Resistance distance = 1 - cosine\\n                    resistance = 1.0 - sim\\n                elif weight_scheme == 'similarity':\\n                    # Use similarity directly as weight (NetworkX will invert)\\n                    resistance = sim\\n                else:\\n                    resistance = 1.0 - sim  # Default: distance\\n                \\n                G.add_edge(i, j, weight=resistance)\\n                edges_added += 1\\n    \\n    print(f'  Semantic graph: {n} nodes, {edges_added} edges (threshold={similarity_threshold})')\\n    return G\\n\\n\\ndef build_sequential_graph(sentences):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Build baseline graph with only sequential edges (i to i+1).\\n    Used for ablation study.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    n = len(sentences)\\n    G = nx.Graph()\\n    for i in range(n):\\n        G.add_node(i)\\n    for i in range(n-1):\\n        G.add_edge(i, i+1, weight=1.0)  # Uniform resistance\\n    return G\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 3: COMPUTE EFFECTIVE RESISTANCE (KIRCHHOFF INDEX)\\n\\n### Step 3.1: Primary Method - NetworkX Built-in\\n```python\\nimport networkx as nx\\nimport numpy as np\\nfrom scipy.linalg import pinv  # For manual computation if needed\\n\\ndef compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Compute Kirchhoff index using NetworkX built-in function.\\n    Returns: Kirchhoff index (scalar)\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    try:\\n        # NetworkX effective_graph_resistance computes Kf = sum of all resistance distances\\n        # This is the Kirchhoff index\\n        kf = nx.effective_graph_resistance(G)\\n        return kf\\n    except Exception as e:\\n        print(f'  NetworkX failed: {e}, falling back to manual computation')\\n        return compute_kirchhoff_index_manual(G)\\n\\n\\ndef compute_kirchhoff_index_manual(G):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Manual computation of Kirchhoff index via Laplacian pseudoinverse.\\n    Kf = n * trace(L^+) where L^+ is pseudoinverse of Laplacian.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    n = G.number_of_nodes()\\n    if n < 2:\\n        return 0.0\\n    \\n    # Get Laplacian matrix\\n    L = nx.laplacian_matrix(G).toarray()  # (n, n)\\n    \\n    # Compute pseudoinverse\\n    L_pinv = pinv(L)\\n    \\n    # Kirchhoff index = n * trace(L_pinv)\\n    kf = n * np.trace(L_pinv)\\n    return kf\\n\\n\\ndef compute_resistance_metric(text, similarity_threshold=0.3, weight_scheme='inverse_cosine', graph_type='semantic'):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Full pipeline: text -> graph -> Kirchhoff index.\\n    \\n    Returns:\\n        kf: Kirchhoff index (effective graph resistance)\\n        n_sentences: Number of sentences\\n        graph: The constructed graph (for debugging)\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    sentences = segment_sentences(text)\\n    n_sentences = len(sentences)\\n    \\n    if n_sentences < 2:\\n        r<response clipped><NOTE>Due to the max output limit, only part of this file has been shown to you. {max(sentence_counts)}]')\\n    print(f'  Mean sentences per text: {np.mean(sentence_counts):.1f}')\\n    \\n    # Step 3: Try different similarity thresholds\\n    print('\\\\n[3] Testing different similarity thresholds...')\\n    for threshold in [0.3, 0.5, 0.7]:\\n        print(f'\\\\n  --- Threshold: {threshold} ---')\\n        # Test on small subset first\\n        test_texts = texts[:50]\\n   "file_text": "EXPERIMENT PLAN: Readability as Circuit Resistance\n=====================================================\n\nOBJECTIVE:\nImplement and evaluate the effective resistance (Kirchhoff index) metric \nwith SBERT semantic edges for text readability assessment.\nAddress ALL major reviewer issues from the first (invalid) experiment.\n\nDATASET:\n- Primary: CLEAR Corpus\n  URL: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/scrosseye/CLEAR-Corpus/main/data/clear_corpus.csv\n  Expected format: CSV with text column (excerpt/text) and readability score column (1-100 scale)\n  \n- Fallback: agentlans/readability dataset (from dependency artifact)\n  Path: ../../iter_1/gen_art/gen_art_dataset_1/full_data_out/full_data_out_1.json\n  Has: 'input' (text), 'output' (grade score 0-20)\n\nMETHOD:\n1. SENTENCE SEGMENTATION\n   - Use nltk.sent_tokenize()\n   - Filter sentences < 10 chars as noise\n   - Download punkt/punkt_tab if needed\n\n2. SBERT EMBEDDINGS\n   - Model: all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (384-dim, fast)\n   - Encode sentences: model.encode(sentences, show_progress_bar=False)\n\n3. BUILD SEMANTIC GRAPH\n   - Compute cosine similarity matrix from normalized embeddings\n   - Add edge if cosine_similarity > threshold (TRY 0.3, 0.5)\n   - Edge weight = 1/(1+cosine) [resistance]\n   - Also build sequential-only graph for ablation\n\n4. COMPUTE KIRCHHOFF INDEX\n   - Primary: nx.effective_graph_resistance(G)\n   - Fallback: manual via Laplacian pseudoinverse\n   - Handle disconnected graphs (connect components or use nx.resistance_distance)\n\n5. NORMALIZATION SCHEMES (try all)\n   - raw: Kf as-is\n   - by_n: Kf/n\n   - by_n_squared: Kf/n^2 (average resistance distance)\n   - by_n_cubed: Kf/(n^2*(n-1))\n\n6. EVALUATION METRICS\n   - Pearson/Spearman correlation with human judgments\n   - MAE/RMSE (fit linear regression to map resistance -> readability)\n   - Number of distinct values (>10 REQUIRED - this was the problem before!)\n   - Partial correlation controlling for sentence count\n   - Bootstrap confidence intervals (1000 resamples)\n\n7. ABLATION STUDY\n   - Compare sequential-only vs semantic edges\n   - Semantic should outperform if discourse structure matters\n\n8. BASELINE COMPARISON\n   - Traditional: Flesch-Kincaid, SMOG, Coleman-Liau (textstat library)\n   - Compare correlations\n\n9. RUNTIME SCALING\n   - Measure time for 10, 50, 100, 500 sentence texts\n   - Verify <1s per document for typical lengths\n\nSUCCESS CRITERIA:\n1. Pearson correlation r > 0.5 with human readability ratings\n2. Number of distinct values > 10 (NOT trivially few!)\n3. Partial correlation (controlling sentence count) > 0.3\n4. Computationally feasible (< 1s per document)\n\nFALLBACK PLAN:\n- If CLEAR corpus fails: use agentlans/readability dataset\n- If SBERT fails: use TF-IDF cosine similarity\n- If graph too large: use k-NN graph (k=5)\n- If Kirchhoff index fails: use average resistance distance\n- If too few distinct values: try different normalization schemes\n- If low correlation: validate dataset with traditional baselines first\n\nTESTING PLAN (run BEFORE full experiment):\n1. test_graph_construction(): verify similar sentences get connected\n2. test_resistance_computation(): verify complete graph < path graph Kf\n3. test_tiny_experiment(): run on 10 synthetic texts, check distinct values > 3\n4. test_clear_corpus_loading(): verify CSV can be downloaded\n5. test_runtime_scaling(): verify <1s for typical lengths\n6. test_distinct_values(): CRITICAL - verify >2 distinct values (was problem before!)\n7. integration_test(): run full pipeline on 50 texts, check ALL reviewer issues\n\nOUTPUT FORMAT (method_out.json):\n{\n  \"experiment_name\": \"readability_circuit_resistance\",\n  \"dataset\": \"CLEAR_corpus\",\n  \"results\": {scheme: {pearson_r, spearman_r, mae, rmse, n_distinct_values, partial_r, n_valid}},\n  \"ablation\": {\"sequential\": {pearson_r, n_distinct}, \"semantic\": {pearson_r, n_distinct}},\n  \"hypothesis_supported\": bool,\n  \"reviewer_issues_addressed\": {\n    \"continuous_values\": bool,\n    \"partial_correlation\": bool,\n    \"semantic_edges\": true,\n    \"established_benchmark\": true\n  }\n}\n\nCOMPUTE PROFILE: cpu_heavy (SBERT embedding computation is CPU-intensive)\n",
  "old_str": null,
  "new_str": null,
  "insert_line": null,
  "view_range": null
}

'excerpt' or 'text': the text passage\\n# - 'readability_score' or 'score': human readability rating (1-100 scale)\\n# If column names differ, executor must map them\\n\\n# Map to standard names\\n# Common CLEAR format: text = excerpt, label = readability score (0-1 or 1-100)\\n# Executor: inspect actual columns and adapt\\ntext_col = 'excerpt'  # or 'text' - check actual\\nlabel_col = 'readability_score'  # or 'score' - check actual\\n\\ntexts = df[text_col].tolist()\\nscores = df[label_col].tolist()  # Human judgments on 1-100 scale\\n\\n# If scores are 0-1, convert to 1-100\\nif max(scores) <= 1.0:\\n    scores = [s * 100 for s in scores]\\n```\\n\\n### Step 1.2: Also Load Dependency Dataset (agentlans/readability)\\n```python\\nimport json\\n\\n# Load the agentlans readability dataset from dependency artifact\\n# This provides additional evaluation data with continuous scores\\nwith open('../../iter_1/gen_art/gen_art_dataset_1/full_data_out/full_data_out_1.json') as f:\\n    agentlans_data = json.load(f)\\n\\n# Use as secondary evaluation set\\n# Has 'input' (text) and 'output' (grade score 0-20)\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 2: GRAPH CONSTRUCTION WITH SBERT SEMANTIC EDGES\\n\\n### Step 2.1: Sentence Segmentation\\n```python\\nimport nltk\\nfrom nltk.tokenize import sent_tokenize\\n\\n# Ensure punkt available\\ntry:\\n    nltk.data.find('tokenizers/punkt')\\nexcept LookupError:\\n    nltk.download('punkt')\\n    nltk.download('punkt_tab')\\n\\ndef segment_sentences(text):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Split text into sentences. Returns list of sentence strings.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    sentences = sent_tokenize(text)\\n    # Filter out very short sentences (likely noise)\\n    sentences = [s.strip() for s in sentences if len(s.strip()) > 10]\\n    return sentences\\n```\\n\\n### Step 2.2: SBERT Embeddings\\n```python\\nfrom sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer\\nimport numpy as np\\n\\n# Load SBERT model (small, fast, good quality)\\nmodel = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')  # 384-dim embeddings\\n\\ndef get_sentence_embeddings(sentences):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Get SBERT embeddings for a list of sentences.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    embeddings = model.encode(sentences, show_progress_bar=False)\\n    return embeddings  # Shape: (n_sentences, 384)\\n```\\n\\n### Step 2.3: Build Discourse Graph with Semantic Edges\\n```python\\nimport networkx as nx\\n\\ndef build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, similarity_threshold=0.3, weight_scheme='inverse_cosine'):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Build discourse graph with SEMANTIC edges based on SBERT cosine similarity.\\n    \\n    Args:\\n        sentences: List of sentence strings\\n        embeddings: SBERT embeddings (n_sentences, 384)\\n        similarity_threshold: Minimum cosine similarity to add edge (try 0.3, 0.5)\\n        weight_scheme: How to convert similarity to resistance\\n            - 'inverse_cosine': weight = 1/(1+cosine) [resistance]\\n            - 'distance': weight = 1 - cosine [resistance distance]\\n            - 'similarity': weight = cosine [conductance]\\n    \\n    Returns:\\n        G: NetworkX graph with semantic edges\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    n = len(sentences)\\n    G = nx.Graph()\\n    \\n    # Add nodes (sentences)\\n    for i, sent in enumerate(sentences):\\n        G.add_node(i, sentence=sent[:50])  # Store first 50 chars as label\\n    \\n    # Compute cosine similarity matrix\\n    # embeddings is (n, 384), normalize to unit vectors\\n    norms = np.linalg.norm(embeddings, axis=1, keepdims=True)\\n    normalized_embeddings = embeddings / (norms + 1e-8)\\n    similarity_matrix = np.dot(normalized_embeddings, normalized_embeddings.T)  # (n, n)\\n    \\n    # Add edges where similarity > threshold\\n    edges_added = 0\\n    for i in range(n):\\n        for j in range(i+1, n):\\n            sim = similarity_matrix[i, j]\\n            if sim > similarity_threshold:\\n                # Convert similarity to resistance (weight = resistance)\\n                if weight_scheme == 'inverse_cosine':\\n                    # Resistance = 1/(1+cosine): high cosine -> low resistance\\n                    resistance = 1.0 / (1.0 + sim)\\n                elif weight_scheme == 'distance':\\n                    # Resistance distance = 1 - cosine\\n                    resistance = 1.0 - sim\\n                elif weight_scheme == 'similarity':\\n                    # Use similarity directly as weight (NetworkX will invert)\\n                    resistance = sim\\n                else:\\n                    resistance = 1.0 - sim  # Default: distance\\n                \\n                G.add_edge(i, j, weight=resistance)\\n                edges_added += 1\\n    \\n    print(f'  Semantic graph: {n} nodes, {edges_added} edges (threshold={similarity_threshold})')\\n    return G\\n\\n\\ndef build_sequential_graph(sentences):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Build baseline graph with only sequential edges (i to i+1).\\n    Used for ablation study.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    n = len(sentences)\\n    G = nx.Graph()\\n    for i in range(n):\\n        G.add_node(i)\\n    for i in range(n-1):\\n        G.add_edge(i, i+1, weight=1.0)  # Uniform resistance\\n    return G\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 3: COMPUTE EFFECTIVE RESISTANCE (KIRCHHOFF INDEX)\\n\\n### Step 3.1: Primary Method - NetworkX Built-in\\n```python\\nimport networkx as nx\\nimport numpy as np\\nfrom scipy.linalg import pinv  # For manual computation if needed\\n\\ndef compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Compute Kirchhoff index using NetworkX built-in function.\\n    Returns: Kirchhoff index (scalar)\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    try:\\n        # NetworkX effective_graph_resistance computes Kf = sum of all resistance distances\\n        # This is the Kirchhoff index\\n        kf = nx.effective_graph_resistance(G)\\n        return kf\\n    except Exception as e:\\n        print(f'  NetworkX failed: {e}, falling back to manual computation')\\n        return compute_kirchhoff_index_manual(G)\\n\\n\\ndef compute_kirchhoff_index_manual(G):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Manual computation of Kirchhoff index via Laplacian pseudoinverse.\\n    Kf = n * trace(L^+) where L^+ is pseudoinverse of Laplacian.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    n = G.number_of_nodes()\\n    if n < 2:\\n        return 0.0\\n    \\n    # Get Laplacian matrix\\n    L = nx.laplacian_matrix(G).toarray()  # (n, n)\\n    \\n    # Compute pseudoinverse\\n    L_pinv = pinv(L)\\n    \\n    # Kirchhoff index = n * trace(L_pinv)\\n    kf = n * np.trace(L_pinv)\\n    return kf\\n\\n\\ndef compute_resistance_metric(text, similarity_threshold=0.3, weight_scheme='inverse_cosine', graph_type='semantic'):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Full pipeline: text -> graph -> Kirchhoff index.\\n    \\n    Returns:\\n        kf: Kirchhoff index (effective graph resistance)\\n        n_sentences: Number of sentences\\n        graph: The constructed graph (for debugging)\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    sentences = segment_sentences(text)\\n    n_sentences = len(sentences)\\n    \\n    if n_sentences < 2:\\n        r",
  "new_str": "{\n  \"title\": \"Readability as Circuit Resistance Experiment\",\n  \"summary\": \"Implement and evaluate effective resistance metric using SBERT semantic edges on CLEAR corpus, addressing all reviewer issues.\",\n  \"runpod_compute_profile\": \"cpu_heavy\",\n  \"implementation_pseudocode\": \"Detailed plan in plan_detailed.txt. Steps: (1) Install packages (sentence-transformers, networkx, pandas, numpy, scipy, scikit-learn, nltk, textstat). (2) Download CLEAR corpus from GitHub. (3) For each text: segment sentences, compute SBERT embeddings, build semantic graph (edges where cosine_sim > 0.3, weight=1/(1+cosine)), compute Kirchhoff index via nx.effective_graph_resistance(). (4) Try normalization schemes: raw Kf, Kf/n, Kf/n^2, Kf/(n^2*(n-1)). (5) Evaluate: Pearson/Spearman correlation, MAE/RMSE, distinct values check (>10 required), partial correlation controlling sentence count, bootstrap CI. (6) Ablation: sequential vs semantic edges. (7) Compare with baselines (Flesch-Kincaid, SMOG). (8) Runtime scaling. (9) Save results to method_out.json.\",\n  \"fallback_plan\": \"If CLEAR corpus fails: use agentlans/readability dataset from dependency artifact. If SBERT fails: use TF-IDF cosine similarity. If graph too large: use k-NN graph (k=5). If Kirchhoff index fails: use average resistance distance. If too few distinct values: try different normalization schemes. If low correlation: validate dataset with traditional baselines first.\",\n  \"testing_plan\": \"Before full experiment, run tests: (1) test_graph_construction(): verify similar sentences connected. (5) Evaluate: Pearson/Spearman correlation, MAE/RMSE, distinct values check (>10 required), partial correlation controlling sentence count, bootstrap CI. (6) Ablation: sequential vs semantic edges. (7) Compare with baselines (Flesch-Kincaid, SMOG). (8) Runtime scaling. (9) Save results to method_out.json.\",\n     6\t  \"fallback_plan\": \"If CLEAR corpus fails: use agentlans/readability dataset from dependency artifact. If SBERT fails: use TF-IDF cosine similarity. If graph too large: use k-NN graph (k=5). If Kirchhoff index fails: use average resistance distance. If too few distinct values: try different normalization schemes. If low correlation: validate dataset with traditional baselines first.\",\n     7\t  \"testing_plan\": \"Before full experiment, run tests: (1) test_graph_construction(): verify similar sentences connected. (2) test_resistance_computation(): verify complete graph has lower Kf than path graph. (3) test_tiny_experiment(): run on 10 synthetic texts, check distinct values > 3. (4) test_clear_corpus_loading(): verify CSV download. (5) test_runtime_scaling(): verify <1s for typical lengths. (6) test_distinct_values(): CRITICAL - verify >2 distinct values (was problem before!). (7) integration_test(): run on 50 texts, check all reviewer issues. Only proceed if ALL tests pass.\"\n     8\t}eturn None, n_sentences, None  # Cannot compute resistance for <2 sentences\\n    \\n    # Get embeddings\\n    embeddings = get_sentence_embeddings(sentences)\\n    \\n    # Build graph\\n    if graph_type == 'semantic':\\n        G = build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, similarity_threshold, weight_scheme)\\n    elif graph_type == 'sequential':\\n        G = build_sequential_graph(sentences)\\n    else:\\n        raise ValueError(f'Unknown graph_type: {graph_type}')\\n    \\n    # Check connectivity (effective resistance is infinite for disconnected graphs)\\n    if not nx.is_connected(G):\\n        # Add edges to connect components (minimum spanning tree on complete graph)\\n        # Alternative: use resistance_distance which handles disconnected graphs\\n        print(f'  Warning: Graph is disconnected ({nx.number_connected_components(G)} components)')\\n        # Connect components by adding edges between closest sentences\\n        G = connect_graph_components(G, sentences, embeddings)\\n    \\n    # Compute Kirchhoff index\\n    kf = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G)\\n    \\n    return kf, n_sentences, G\\n\\n\\ndef connect_graph_components(G, sentences, embeddings):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Connect disconnected components by adding edges between closest sentences\\n    (measured by cosine similarity of embeddings).\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    components = list(nx.connected_components(G))\\n    if len(components) <= 1:\\n        return G\\n    \\n    # For each pair of components, find the pair of nodes with highest similarity\\n    # and add an edge between them\\n    while len(list(nx.connected_components(G))) > 1:\\n        comps = list(nx.connected_components(G))\\n        best_sim = -1\\n        best_pair = None\\n        \\n        for i, comp_a in enumerate(comps):\\n            for comp_b in comps[i+1:]:\\n                for a in comp_a:\\n                    for b in comp_b:\\n                        sim = np.dot(embeddings[a], embeddings[b]) / (np.linalg.norm(embeddings[a]) * np.linalg.norm(embeddings[b]) + 1e-8)\\n                        if sim > best_sim:\\n                            best_sim = sim\\n                            best_pair = (a, b)\\n        \\n        if best_pair:\\n            G.add_edge(best_pair[0], best_pair[1], weight=1.0 / (1.0 + best_sim))\\n    \\n    return G\\n```\\n\\n### Step 3.2: Multiple Normalization Schemes\\n```python\\ndef normalize_kirchhoff_index(kf, n_sentences, scheme='raw'):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Normalize Kirchhoff index for comparability across texts.\\n    \\n    Schemes:\\n    - 'raw': Kf as-is\\n    - 'by_n': Kf / n (average resistance distance * n)\\n    - 'by_n_squared': Kf / (n^2) (average resistance distance)\\n    - 'by_n_cubed': Kf / (n^2 * (n-1)) (theoretical: resistance distance scales with n)\\n    - 'log': log(Kf) (if Kf spans multiple orders of magnitude)\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if kf is None or n_sentences < 2:\\n        return None\\n    \\n    n = n_sentences\\n    \\n    if scheme == 'raw':\\n        return kf\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n':\\n        return kf / n\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n_squared':\\n        return kf / (n ** 2)\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n_cubed':\\n        return kf / (n ** 2 * (n - 1)) if n > 1 else kf\\n    elif scheme == 'log':\\n        return np.log(kf + 1e-8)  # Add small epsilon to avoid log(0)\\n    else:\\n        return kf\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 4: COMPREHENSIVE EVALUATION\\n\\n### Step 4.1: Main Evaluation on CLEAR Corpus\\n```python\\nimport pandas as pd\\nimport numpy as np\\nfrom scipy import stats\\nfrom sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error\\nimport json\\n\\n\\ndef evaluate_readability_metric(texts, human_scores, output_prefix=''):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Comprehensive evaluation of the resistance metric.\\n    \\n    Args:\\n        texts: List of text passages\\n        human_scores: List of human readability ratings (1-100 scale)\\n        output_prefix: Prefix for output files\\n    \\n    Returns:\\n        results: Dict with all evaluation metrics\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    results = {}\\n    \\n    # Compute resistance scores for all texts\\n    resistance_scores = {'raw': [], 'by_n': [], 'by_n_squared': [], 'by_n_cubed': []}\\n    n_sentences_list = []\\n    valid_indices = []  # Track which texts produced valid scores\\n    \\n    for idx, text in enumerate(texts):\\n        try:\\n            kf, n_sent, G = compute_resistance_metric(\\n                text, \\n                similarity_threshold=0.3, \\n                weight_scheme='inverse_cosine',\\n                graph_type='semantic'\\n            )\\n            \\n            if kf is not None and n_sent >= 2:\\n                valid_indices.append(idx)\\n                n_sentences_list.append(n_sent)\\n                \\n                # Multiple normalization schemes\\n                resistance_scores['raw'].append(kf)\\n                resistance_scores['by_n'].append(kf / n_sent)\\n                resistance_scores['by_n_squared'].append(kf / (n_sent ** 2))\\n                resistance_scores['by_n_cubed'].append(kf / (n_sent ** 2 * (n_sent - 1)) if n_sent > 1 else kf)\\n            else:\\n                print(f'  Skipping text {idx}: {n_sent} sentences (need >= 2)')\\n        except Exception as e:\\n            print(f'  Error processing text {idx}: {e}')\\n    \\n    # Filter human scores to valid indices\\n    valid_human_scores = [human_scores[i] for i in valid_indices]\\n    \\n    print(f'\\\\n=== EVALUATION RESULTS ===')\\n    print(f'Valid texts: {len(valid_indices)} / {len(texts)}')\\n    \\n    # Evaluate each normalization scheme\\n    for scheme in resistance_scores:\\n        scores = resistance_scores[scheme]\\n        if len(scores) < 10:\\n            print(f'  {scheme}: Not enough valid scores')\\n            continue\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 1: Number of distinct values (>10 required)\\n        n_distinct = len(set(scores))\\n        print(f'\\\\n--- Scheme: {scheme} ---')\\n        p<response clipped><NOTE>Due to the max output limit, only part of this file has been shown to you. If results look reasonable, proceed to full experiment.')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T4: Validate CLEAR Corpus Loading\\n```python\\ndef test_clear_corpus_loading():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Test that CLEAR corpus can be downloaded and parsed.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TEST: CLEAR Corpus Loading ===')\\n    \\n    try:\\n        url = 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/scrosseye/CLEAR-Corpus/main/data/clear_corpus.csv'\\n        df = pd.read_csv(url)\\n        \\n        print(f'SUCCESS: Loaded {len(df)} rows')\\n        print(f'Columns: {df.columns.tolist()}')\\n        print(f'First 3 rows:')\\n        print(df.head(3))\\n        \\n        # Check for required columns\\n        text_col = None\\n        score_col = None\\n        \\n        for col in df.columns:\\n            if col.lower() in ['excerpt', 'text', 'passage']:\\n                text_col = col\\n            if 'readability' in col.lower() or 'score' in col.lower():\\n                score_col = col\\n        \\n        if text_col and score_col:\\n            print(f'SUCCESS: Found text column \\\"{text_col}\\\" and score column \\\"{score_col}\\\"')\\n            print(f'Score range: [{df[score_col].min()}, {df[score_col].max()}]')\\n            \\n            # Check a sample text\\n            sample_text = df[text_col].iloc[0]\\n            print(f'Sample text (first 100 chars): {sample_text[:100]}')\\n            \\n            return True\\n        else:\\n            print('WARNING: Could not identify text/score columns')\\n            print('Executor must manually inspect and specify column names')\\n            return False\\n            \\n    except Exception as e:\\n        print(f'FAILED: {e}')\\n        print('Will use fallback dataset (agentlans/readability)')\\n        return False\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T5: Runtime Test\\n```python\\ndef test_runtime_scaling():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Verify that runtime is acceptable for typical text lengths.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TEST: Runtime Scaling ===')\\n    \\n    test_cases = [\\n        ('Short (5 sentences)', 'This is a test. It has few sentences. Not very long. But it works. The end.'),\\n        ('Medium (10 sentences)', 'Sentence one. Sentence two. Sentence three. Sentence four. Sentence five. Sentence six. Sentence seven. Sentence eight. Sentence nine. Sentence ten.'),\\n        ('Long (20 sentences)', 'S1. ' * 20),  # Will be processed as 20 sentences\\n    ]\\n    \\n    for name, text in test_cases:\\n        start = time.time()\\n        kf, n, G = compute_resistance_metric(text, similarity_threshold=0.3)\\n        elapsed = time.time() - start\\n        \\n        print(f'{name}: n={n}, time={elapsed:.3f}s, Kf={kf:.2f}')\\n        \\n        if elapsed > 5.0:\\n            print(f'  WARNING: Slow! {elapsed:.1f}s for {n} sentences')\\n            print(f'  SUGGESTION: Use k-NN graph or increase similarity threshold')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T6: Validate Distinct Values Requirement\\n```python\\ndef test_distinct_values():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"CRITICAL TEST: Verify metric produces enough distinct values.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TEST: Distinct Values (Reviewer Issue #1) ===')\\n    \\n    # Load a few diverse texts from CLEAR corpus (or use test texts)\\n    test_texts = [\\n        \\\"Short simple text. Few sentences.\\\",  # Very short\\n        \\\"This is a medium length text. It has a few more sentences. Each sentence is moderately complex. The ideas are somewhat connected.\\\",  # Medium\\n        \\\"The epistemological ramifications of quantum decoherence necessitate a paradigmatic reconceptualization of macroscopic realism that transcends classical metaphysical frameworks.\\\" * 5,  # Very complex, repeated\\n        \\\"Alice went to the store. Bob went to the park. They met at the cafe. The cafe was closed. They went home.\\\" * 3,  # Narratives\\n    ]\\n    \\n    for scheme in ['raw', 'by_n', 'by_n_squared']:\\n        scores = []\\n        for text in test_texts:\\n            kf, n, G = compute_resistance_metric(text, threshold=0.3)\\n            if kf:\\n                normalized = normalize_kirchhoff_index(kf, n, scheme)\\n                scores.append(normalized)\\n        \\n        n_distinct = len(set([round(s, 4) for s in scores]))\\n        print(f'Scheme \\\"{scheme}\\\": {n_distinct} distinct values from {len(scores)} texts')\\n        \\n        if n_distinct <= 2:\\n            print('  CRITICAL FAILURE: Metric produces too few distinct values!')\\n            print('  This was the problem with the first experiment.')\\n            print('  DEBUG: Check graph construction - are all graphs identical?')\\n            print('  DEBUG: Check normalization - is division by n^2 collapsing values?')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T7: Integration Test (Full Pipeline on 50 Texts)\\n```python\\ndef integration_test():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Run full pipeline on 50 texts to catch integration issues.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== INTEGRATION TEST (50 texts) ===')\\n    \\n    # Try to load CLEAR corpus\\n    if test_clear_corpus_loading():\\n        # Use actual CLEAR data\\n        df = pd.read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/scrosseye/CLEAR-Corpus/main/data/clear_corpus.csv')\\n        texts = df['excerpt'].tolist()[:50]\\n        scores = df['readability_score'].tolist()[:50]\\n    else:\\n        # Use synthetic test data\\n        texts = [f'Test text number {i}. With some sentences. And ideas.' for i in range(50)]\\n        scores = list(range(50))  # Synthetic scores 0-49\\n    \\n    # Run full evaluation\\n    results, valid_idx = evaluate_readability_metric(texts, scores)\\n    \\n    # Check all reviewer issues\\n    print('\\\\n--- REVIEWER ISSUES CHECKLIST ---')\\n    \\n    for scheme, res in results.items():\\n        print(f'\\\\nScheme: {scheme}')\\n        \\n        # Issue 1: Continuous values\\n        print(f'  [1] Distinct values: {res[\\\"n_distinct_values\\\"]} (need >10: {\\\"PASS\\\" if res[\\\"n_distinct_values\\\"] > 10 else \\\"FAIL\\\"})')\\n        \\n        # Issue 2: Partial correlation\\n        print(f'  [2] Partial r (control sent count): {res[\\\"partial_r\\\"]:.4f} (need >0: {\\\"PASS\\\" if res[\\\"partial_r\\\"] > 0 else \\\"FAIL\\\"})')\\n        \\n        # Issue 3: Semantic edges\\n        print(f'  [3] Using semantic edges: True (by design)')\\n        \\n        # Issue 4: Established benchmark\\n        print(f'  [4] Using CLEAR corpus: True')\\n    \\n    print('\\\\nIntegration test complete. Review results before running full experiment.')\\n```\\n\\n### Test Execution Order\\n```\\n1. test_graph_construction()          # Unit test: graph building works\\n2. test_resistance_computation()     # Unit test: Kf computation works  \\n3. test_clear_corpus_loading()       # Integration: can load evaluation data\\n4. test_distinct_values()            # CRITICAL: metric isn't trivial\\n5. test_tiny_experiment()            # Integration: full pipeline on N=10\\n6. test_runtime_scaling()            # Performance: runtime acceptable?\\n7. integration_test()                # Full: 50 texts, all metrics\\n\\nOnly proceed to full experiment if ALL tests pass.\\n```\"\n     9\t}\n    10\t\nReview the changes and make sure they are as expected. Edit the file again if necessary."}], "is_error": false, "command": "str_replace", "path": "/ai-inventor/aii_data/runs/run_RiHQW43yNEvO/3_invention_loop/iter_2/gen_plan/gen_plan_experiment_1/.sdk_openhands_agent_struct_out.json", "prev_exist": true, "old_content": "{\n  \"title\": \"Readability as Circuit Resistance Experiment\",\n  \"summary\": \"Implement and evaluate effective resistance metric using SBERT semantic edges on CLEAR corpus, addressing all reviewer issues including continuous values, partial correlation, and ablation comparisons.\",\n  \"runpod_compute_profile\": \"cpu_heavy\",\n  \"implementation_pseudocode\": \"## PHASE 0: ENVIRONMENT SETUP\\n\\n```python\\n# Install required packages\\n# uv pip install sentence-transformers networkx pandas numpy scipy scikit-learn nltk\\n# python -m nltk.downloader punkt punkt_tab\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 1: DATA ACQUISITION\\n\\n### Step 1.1: Download CLEAR Corpus\\n```python\\nimport pandas as pd\\nimport requests\\n\\n# Download CLEAR corpus CSV\\nclear_url = 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/scrosseye/CLEAR-Corpus/main/data/clear_corpus.csv'\\ndf = pd.read_csv(clear_url)\\n\\n# Inspect columns (executor must adapt to actual column names)\\n# Expected columns from research: text/excerpt, readability_score, maybe genre\\nprint(df.columns.tolist())\\nprint(df.head())\\nprint(f'Shape: {df.shape}')\\n\\n# The CLEAR corpus typically has:\\n# - 'excerpt' or 'text': the text passage\\n# - 'readability_score' or 'score': human readability rating (1-100 scale)\\n# If column names differ, executor must map them\\n\\n# Map to standard names\\n# Common CLEAR format: text = excerpt, label = readability score (0-1 or 1-100)\\n# Executor: inspect actual columns and adapt\\ntext_col = 'excerpt'  # or 'text' - check actual\\nlabel_col = 'readability_score'  # or 'score' - check actual\\n\\ntexts = df[text_col].tolist()\\nscores = df[label_col].tolist()  # Human judgments on 1-100 scale\\n\\n# If scores are 0-1, convert to 1-100\\nif max(scores) <= 1.0:\\n    scores = [s * 100 for s in scores]\\n```\\n\\n### Step 1.2: Also Load Dependency Dataset (agentlans/readability)\\n```python\\nimport json\\n\\n# Load the agentlans readability dataset from dependency artifact\\n# This provides additional evaluation data with continuous scores\\nwith open('../../iter_1/gen_art/gen_art_dataset_1/full_data_out/full_data_out_1.json') as f:\\n    agentlans_data = json.load(f)\\n\\n# Use as secondary evaluation set\\n# Has 'input' (text) and 'output' (grade score 0-20)\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 2: GRAPH CONSTRUCTION WITH SBERT SEMANTIC EDGES\\n\\n### Step 2.1: Sentence Segmentation\\n```python\\nimport nltk\\nfrom nltk.tokenize import sent_tokenize\\n\\n# Ensure punkt available\\ntry:\\n    nltk.data.find('tokenizers/punkt')\\nexcept LookupError:\\n    nltk.download('punkt')\\n    nltk.download('punkt_tab')\\n\\ndef segment_sentences(text):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Split text into sentences. Returns list of sentence strings.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    sentences = sent_tokenize(text)\\n    # Filter out very short sentences (likely noise)\\n    sentences = [s.strip() for s in sentences if len(s.strip()) > 10]\\n    return sentences\\n```\\n\\n### Step 2.2: SBERT Embeddings\\n```python\\nfrom sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer\\nimport numpy as np\\n\\n# Load SBERT model (small, fast, good quality)\\nmodel = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')  # 384-dim embeddings\\n\\ndef get_sentence_embeddings(sentences):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Get SBERT embeddings for a list of sentences.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    embeddings = model.encode(sentences, show_progress_bar=False)\\n    return embeddings  # Shape: (n_sentences, 384)\\n```\\n\\n### Step 2.3: Build Discourse Graph with Semantic Edges\\n```python\\nimport networkx as nx\\n\\ndef build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, similarity_threshold=0.3, weight_scheme='inverse_cosine'):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Build discourse graph with SEMANTIC edges based on SBERT cosine similarity.\\n    \\n    Args:\\n        sentences: List of sentence strings\\n        embeddings: SBERT embeddings (n_sentences, 384)\\n        similarity_threshold: Minimum cosine similarity to add edge (try 0.3, 0.5)\\n        weight_scheme: How to convert similarity to resistance\\n            - 'inverse_cosine': weight = 1/(1+cosine) [resistance]\\n            - 'distance': weight = 1 - cosine [resistance distance]\\n            - 'similarity': weight = cosine [conductance]\\n    \\n    Returns:\\n        G: NetworkX graph with semantic edges\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    n = len(sentences)\\n    G = nx.Graph()\\n    \\n    # Add nodes (sentences)\\n    for i, sent in enumerate(sentences):\\n        G.add_node(i, sentence=sent[:50])  # Store first 50 chars as label\\n    \\n    # Compute cosine similarity matrix\\n    # embeddings is (n, 384), normalize to unit vectors\\n    norms = np.linalg.norm(embeddings, axis=1, keepdims=True)\\n    normalized_embeddings = embeddings / (norms + 1e-8)\\n    similarity_matrix = np.dot(normalized_embeddings, normalized_embeddings.T)  # (n, n)\\n    \\n    # Add edges where similarity > threshold\\n    edges_added = 0\\n    for i in range(n):\\n        for j in range(i+1, n):\\n            sim = similarity_matrix[i, j]\\n            if sim > similarity_threshold:\\n                # Convert similarity to resistance (weight = resistance)\\n                if weight_scheme == 'inverse_cosine':\\n                    # Resistance = 1/(1+cosine): high cosine -> low resistance\\n                    resistance = 1.0 / (1.0 + sim)\\n                elif weight_scheme == 'distance':\\n                    # Resistance distance = 1 - cosine\\n                    resistance = 1.0 - sim\\n                elif weight_scheme == 'similarity':\\n                    # Use similarity directly as weight (NetworkX will invert)\\n                    resistance = sim\\n                else:\\n                    resistance = 1.0 - sim  # Default: distance\\n                \\n                G.add_edge(i, j, weight=resistance)\\n                edges_added += 1\\n    \\n    print(f'  Semantic graph: {n} nodes, {edges_added} edges (threshold={similarity_threshold})')\\n    return G\\n\\n\\ndef build_sequential_graph(sentences):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Build baseline graph with only sequential edges (i to i+1).\\n    Used for ablation study.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    n = len(sentences)\\n    G = nx.Graph()\\n    for i in range(n):\\n        G.add_node(i)\\n    for i in range(n-1):\\n        G.add_edge(i, i+1, weight=1.0)  # Uniform resistance\\n    return G\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 3: COMPUTE EFFECTIVE RESISTANCE (KIRCHHOFF INDEX)\\n\\n### Step 3.1: Primary Method - NetworkX Built-in\\n```python\\nimport networkx as nx\\nimport numpy as np\\nfrom scipy.linalg import pinv  # For manual computation if needed\\n\\ndef compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Compute Kirchhoff index using NetworkX built-in function.\\n    Returns: Kirchhoff index (scalar)\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    try:\\n        # NetworkX effective_graph_resistance computes Kf = sum of all resistance distances\\n        # This is the Kirchhoff index\\n        kf = nx.effective_graph_resistance(G)\\n        return kf\\n    except Exception as e:\\n        print(f'  NetworkX failed: {e}, falling back to manual computation')\\n        return compute_kirchhoff_index_manual(G)\\n\\n\\ndef compute_kirchhoff_index_manual(G):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Manual computation of Kirchhoff index via Laplacian pseudoinverse.\\n    Kf = n * trace(L^+) where L^+ is pseudoinverse of Laplacian.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    n = G.number_of_nodes()\\n    if n < 2:\\n        return 0.0\\n    \\n    # Get Laplacian matrix\\n    L = nx.laplacian_matrix(G).toarray()  # (n, n)\\n    \\n    # Compute pseudoinverse\\n    L_pinv = pinv(L)\\n    \\n    # Kirchhoff index = n * trace(L_pinv)\\n    kf = n * np.trace(L_pinv)\\n    return kf\\n\\n\\ndef compute_resistance_metric(text, similarity_threshold=0.3, weight_scheme='inverse_cosine', graph_type='semantic'):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Full pipeline: text -> graph -> Kirchhoff index.\\n    \\n    Returns:\\n        kf: Kirchhoff index (effective graph resistance)\\n        n_sentences: Number of sentences\\n        graph: The constructed graph (for debugging)\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    sentences = segment_sentences(text)\\n    n_sentences = len(sentences)\\n    \\n    if n_sentences < 2:\\n        return None, n_sentences, None  # Cannot compute resistance for <2 sentences\\n    \\n    # Get embeddings\\n    embeddings = get_sentence_embeddings(sentences)\\n    \\n    # Build graph\\n    if graph_type == 'semantic':\\n        G = build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, similarity_threshold, weight_scheme)\\n    elif graph_type == 'sequential':\\n        G = build_sequential_graph(sentences)\\n    else:\\n        raise ValueError(f'Unknown graph_type: {graph_type}')\\n    \\n    # Check connectivity (effective resistance is infinite for disconnected graphs)\\n    if not nx.is_connected(G):\\n        # Add edges to connect components (minimum spanning tree on complete graph)\\n        # Alternative: use resistance_distance which handles disconnected graphs\\n        print(f'  Warning: Graph is disconnected ({nx.number_connected_components(G)} components)')\\n        # Connect components by adding edges between closest sentences\\n        G = connect_graph_components(G, sentences, embeddings)\\n    \\n    # Compute Kirchhoff index\\n    kf = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G)\\n    \\n    return kf, n_sentences, G\\n\\n\\ndef connect_graph_components(G, sentences, embeddings):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Connect disconnected components by adding edges between closest sentences\\n    (measured by cosine similarity of embeddings).\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    components = list(nx.connected_components(G))\\n    if len(components) <= 1:\\n        return G\\n    \\n    # For each pair of components, find the pair of nodes with highest similarity\\n    # and add an edge between them\\n    while len(list(nx.connected_components(G))) > 1:\\n        comps = list(nx.connected_components(G))\\n        best_sim = -1\\n        best_pair = None\\n        \\n        for i, comp_a in enumerate(comps):\\n            for comp_b in comps[i+1:]:\\n                for a in comp_a:\\n                    for b in comp_b:\\n                        sim = np.dot(embeddings[a], embeddings[b]) / (np.linalg.norm(embeddings[a]) * np.linalg.norm(embeddings[b]) + 1e-8)\\n                        if sim > best_sim:\\n                            best_sim = sim\\n                            best_pair = (a, b)\\n        \\n        if best_pair:\\n            G.add_edge(best_pair[0], best_pair[1], weight=1.0 / (1.0 + best_sim))\\n    \\n    return G\\n```\\n\\n### Step 3.2: Multiple Normalization Schemes\\n```python\\ndef normalize_kirchhoff_index(kf, n_sentences, scheme='raw'):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Normalize Kirchhoff index for comparability across texts.\\n    \\n    Schemes:\\n    - 'raw': Kf as-is\\n    - 'by_n': Kf / n (average resistance distance * n)\\n    - 'by_n_squared': Kf / (n^2) (average resistance distance)\\n    - 'by_n_cubed': Kf / (n^2 * (n-1)) (theoretical: resistance distance scales with n)\\n    - 'log': log(Kf) (if Kf spans multiple orders of magnitude)\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if kf is None or n_sentences < 2:\\n        return None\\n    \\n    n = n_sentences\\n    \\n    if scheme == 'raw':\\n        return kf\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n':\\n        return kf / n\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n_squared':\\n        return kf / (n ** 2)\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n_cubed':\\n        return kf / (n ** 2 * (n - 1)) if n > 1 else kf\\n    elif scheme == 'log':\\n        return np.log(kf + 1e-8)  # Add small epsilon to avoid log(0)\\n    else:\\n        return kf\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 4: COMPREHENSIVE EVALUATION\\n\\n### Step 4.1: Main Evaluation on CLEAR Corpus\\n```python\\nimport pandas as pd\\nimport numpy as np\\nfrom scipy import stats\\nfrom sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error\\nimport json\\n\\n\\ndef evaluate_readability_metric(texts, human_scores, output_prefix=''):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Comprehensive evaluation of the resistance metric.\\n    \\n    Args:\\n        texts: List of text passages\\n        human_scores: List of human readability ratings (1-100 scale)\\n        output_prefix: Prefix for output files\\n    \\n    Returns:\\n        results: Dict with all evaluation metrics\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    results = {}\\n    \\n    # Compute resistance scores for all texts\\n    resistance_scores = {'raw': [], 'by_n': [], 'by_n_squared': [], 'by_n_cubed': []}\\n    n_sentences_list = []\\n    valid_indices = []  # Track which texts produced valid scores\\n    \\n    for idx, text in enumerate(texts):\\n        try:\\n            kf, n_sent, G = compute_resistance_metric(\\n                text, \\n                similarity_threshold=0.3, \\n                weight_scheme='inverse_cosine',\\n                graph_type='semantic'\\n            )\\n            \\n            if kf is not None and n_sent >= 2:\\n                valid_indices.append(idx)\\n                n_sentences_list.append(n_sent)\\n                \\n                # Multiple normalization schemes\\n                resistance_scores['raw'].append(kf)\\n                resistance_scores['by_n'].append(kf / n_sent)\\n                resistance_scores['by_n_squared'].append(kf / (n_sent ** 2))\\n                resistance_scores['by_n_cubed'].append(kf / (n_sent ** 2 * (n_sent - 1)) if n_sent > 1 else kf)\\n            else:\\n                print(f'  Skipping text {idx}: {n_sent} sentences (need >= 2)')\\n        except Exception as e:\\n            print(f'  Error processing text {idx}: {e}')\\n    \\n    # Filter human scores to valid indices\\n    valid_human_scores = [human_scores[i] for i in valid_indices]\\n    \\n    print(f'\\\\n=== EVALUATION RESULTS ===')\\n    print(f'Valid texts: {len(valid_indices)} / {len(texts)}')\\n    \\n    # Evaluate each normalization scheme\\n    for scheme in resistance_scores:\\n        scores = resistance_scores[scheme]\\n        if len(scores) < 10:\\n            print(f'  {scheme}: Not enough valid scores')\\n            continue\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 1: Number of distinct values (>10 required)\\n        n_distinct = len(set(scores))\\n        print(f'\\\\n--- Scheme: {scheme} ---')\\n        print(f'  Distinct values: {n_distinct}')\\n        \\n        if n_distinct <= 10:\\n            print(f'  WARNING: Only {n_distinct} distinct values! Metric may be trivial.')\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 2: Pearson correlation with human judgments\\n        pearson_r, pearson_p = stats.pearsonr(scores, valid_human_scores)\\n        print(f'  Pearson r: {pearson_r:.4f} (p={pearson_p:.4f})')\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 3: Spearman correlation (rank-based)\\n        spearman_r, spearman_p = stats.spearmanr(scores, valid_human_scores)\\n        print(f'  Spearman r: {spearman_r:.4f} (p={spearman_p:.4f})')\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 4: MAE/RMSE on original 1-100 scale\\n        # First, fit a simple linear regression to map resistance -> readability score\\n        from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression\\n        X = np.array(scores).reshape(-1, 1)\\n        y = np.array(valid_human_scores)\\n        \\n        reg = LinearRegression().fit(X, y)\\n        y_pred = reg.predict(X)\\n        \\n        mae = mean_absolute_error(y, y_pred)\\n        rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y, y_pred))\\n        print(f'  MAE: {mae:.2f}')\\n        print(f'  RMSE: {rmse:.2f}')\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 5: Partial correlation controlling for sentence count\\n        # Does resistance explain variance BEYOND just sentence count?\\n        n_sent_array = np.array([n_sentences_list[i] for i in range(len(scores))])\\n        \\n        # Compute partial correlation: corr(resistance, human) controlling for n_sentences\\n        # Method: residualize both variables on n_sentences, then correlate residuals\\n        X_control = n_sent_array.reshape(-1, 1)\\n        \\n        # Residualize resistance scores\\n        reg_resist = LinearRegression().fit(X_control, scores)\\n        resid_resist = scores - reg_resist.predict(X_control)\\n        \\n        # Residualize human scores\\n        reg_human = LinearRegression().fit(X_control, valid_human_scores)\\n        resid_human = valid_human_scores - reg_human.predict(X_control)\\n        \\n        partial_r, partial_p = stats.pearsonr(resid_resist, resid_human)\\n        print(f'  Partial r (controlling for sentence count): {partial_r:.4f} (p={partial_p:.4f})')\\n        \\n        # Store results\\n        results[scheme] = {\\n            'n_distinct_values': n_distinct,\\n            'pearson_r': pearson_r,\\n            'pearson_p': pearson_p,\\n            'spearman_r': spearman_r,\\n            'spearman_p': spearman_p,\\n            'mae': mae,\\n            'rmse': rmse,\\n            'partial_r': partial_r,\\n            'partial_p': partial_p,\\n            'n_valid': len(scores),\\n            'resistance_scores': scores[:100],  # Store first 100 for analysis\\n            'human_scores': valid_human_scores[:100]\\n        }\\n    \\n    return results, valid_indices\\n```\\n\\n### Step 4.2: Ablation - Sequential vs Semantic Edges\\n```python\\ndef ablation_sequential_vs_semantic(texts, human_scores):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Compare sequential-only graph vs semantic edge graph.\\n    Tests whether semantic edges add value beyond just sentence count.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    print('\\\\n=== ABLATION: Sequential vs Semantic Edges ===')\\n    \\n    results_ablation = {}\\n    \\n    for graph_type in ['sequential', 'semantic']:\\n        scores = []\\n        n_sents = []\\n        valid_indices = []\\n        \\n        for idx, text in enumerate(texts[:500]):  # Subset for speed\\n            try:\\n                kf, n_sent, G = compute_resistance_metric(\\n                    text,\\n                    similarity_threshold=0.3,\\n                    weight_scheme='inverse_cosine',\\n                    graph_type=graph_type\\n                )\\n                if kf is not None and n_sent >= 2:\\n                    scores.append(kf / (n_sent ** 2))  # Normalized\\n                    n_sents.append(n_sent)\\n                    valid_indices.append(idx)\\n            except:\\n                pass\\n        \\n        valid_human = [human_scores[i] for i in valid_indices]\\n        \\n        if len(scores) >= 10:\\n            r, p = stats.pearsonr(scores, valid_human)\\n            n_distinct = len(set([round(s, 4) for s in scores]))\\n            \\n            print(f'  {graph_type}: r={r:.4f}, distinct_values={n_distinct}, n={len(scores)}')\\n            \\n            results_ablation[graph_type] = {\\n                'pearson_r': r,\\n                'pearson_p': p,\\n                'n_distinct_values': n_distinct,\\n                'n_valid': len(scores)\\n            }\\n    \\n    return results_ablation\\n```\\n\\n### Step 4.3: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals\\n```python\\ndef bootstrap_correlation(scores, human_scores, n_bootstrap=1000):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Compute bootstrap confidence interval for Pearson correlation.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    n = len(scores)\\n    bootstrap_rs = []\\n    \\n    for _ in range(n_bootstrap):\\n        # Resample with replacement\\n        indices = np.random.choice(n, size=n, replace=True)\\n        sample_scores = [scores[i] for i in indices]\\n        sample_human = [human_scores[i] for i in indices]\\n        \\n        r, _ = stats.pearsonr(sample_scores, sample_human)\\n        bootstrap_rs.append(r)\\n    \\n    # Compute 95% confidence interval\\n    ci_lower = np.percentile(bootstrap_rs, 2.5)\\n    ci_upper = np.percentile(bootstrap_rs, 97.5)\\n    \\n    return ci_lower, ci_upper, bootstrap_rs\\n```\\n\\n### Step 4.4: Runtime Scaling Analysis\\n```python\\nimport time\\n\\ndef runtime_scaling_analysis(texts_by_length):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Measure runtime for different numbers of sentences.\\n    texts_by_length: Dict mapping n_sentences -> list of texts\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    print('\\\\n=== RUNTIME SCALING ANALYSIS ===')\\n    \\n    results_runtime = {}\\n    \\n    for n_target in [10, 50, 100, 500]:\\n        # Find texts with approximately n_target sentences\\n        for text in texts_by_length.get(n_target, []):\\n            sentences = segment_sentences(text)\\n            if abs(len(sentences) - n_target) < 5:\\n                # Time the computation\\n                start = time.time()\\n                kf, n, G = compute_resistance_metric(text)\\n                elapsed = time.time() - start\\n                \\n                print(f'  n={len(sentences)}: {elapsed:.3f}s')\\n                results_runtime[n_target] = elapsed\\n                break\\n    \\n    return results_runtime\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 5: COMPARE WITH BASELINES\\n\\n### Step 5.1: Traditional Readability Formulas\\n```python\\nimport textstat  # Install with: uv pip install textstat\\n\\ndef compute_traditional_readability(text):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Compute traditional readability metrics for comparison.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    metrics = {}\\n    \\n    try:\\n        metrics['flesch_kincaid_grade'] = textstat.flesch_kincaid_grade(text)\\n    except:\\n        metrics['flesch_kincaid_grade'] = None\\n    \\n    try:\\n        metrics['smog_index'] = textstat.smog_index(text)\\n    except:\\n        metrics['smog_index'] = None\\n    \\n    try:\\n        metrics['coleman_liau_index'] = textstat.coleman_liau_index(text)\\n    except:\\n        metrics['coleman_liau_index'] = None\\n    \\n    try:\\n        metrics['automated_readability_index'] = textstat.automated_readability_index(text)\\n    except:\\n        metrics['automated_readability_index'] = None\\n    \\n    return metrics\\n\\n\\ndef compare_with_baselines(texts, human_scores):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Compare resistance metric with traditional baselines.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    print('\\\\n=== COMPARISON WITH BASELINES ===')\\n    \\n    baseline_scores = {name: [] for name in ['flesch_kincaid_grade', 'smog_index', 'coleman_liau_index']}\\n    valid_indices = []\\n    \\n    for idx, text in enumerate(texts[:500]):\\n        metrics = compute_traditional_readability(text)\\n        valid = True\\n        for name in baseline_scores:\\n            if metrics[name] is None:\\n                valid = False\\n                break\\n        if valid:\\n            valid_indices.append(idx)\\n            for name in baseline_scores:\\n                baseline_scores[name].append(metrics[name])\\n    \\n    valid_human = [human_scores[i] for i in valid_indices]\\n    \\n    for name in baseline_scores:\\n        if len(baseline_scores[name]) >= 10:\\n            r, p = stats.pearsonr(baseline_scores[name], valid_human)\\n            print(f'  {name}: r={r:.4f} (p={p:.4f}), n={len(baseline_scores[name])}')\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 6: MAIN EXECUTION SCRIPT\\n\\n### Step 6.1: Complete Experiment Script\\n```python\\n#!/usr/bin/env python3\\n\\\"\\\"\\\"\\nMain experiment script for Readability as Circuit Resistance.\\nExecutes the full evaluation pipeline on CLEAR corpus.\\n\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n\\nimport json\\nimport pandas as pd\\nimport numpy as np\\nfrom scipy import stats\\nfrom sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error\\nfrom sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression\\nimport warnings\\nwarnings.filterwarnings('ignore')\\n\\n\\ndef main():\\n    print('=' * 60)\\n    print('READABILITY AS CIRCUIT RESISTANCE - FULL EXPERIMENT')\\n    print('=' * 60)\\n    \\n    # Step 1: Load CLEAR Corpus\\n    print('\\\\n[1] Loading CLEAR Corpus...')\\n    clear_url = 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/scrosseye/CLEAR-Corpus/main/data/clear_corpus.csv'\\n    \\n    try:\\n        df = pd.read_csv(clear_url)\\n        print(f'  Loaded {len(df)} texts from CLEAR
