' * 20),  # Will be processed as 20 sentences\\n    ]\\n    \\n    for name, text in test_cases:\\n        start = time.time()\\n        kf, n, G = compute_resistance_metric(text, similarity_threshold=0.3)\\n        elapsed = time.time() - start\\n        \\n        print(f'{name}: n={n}, time={elapsed:.3f}s, Kf={kf:.2f}')\\n        \\n        if elapsed > 5.0:\\n            print(f'  WARNING: Slow! {elapsed:.1f}s for {n} sentences')\\n            print(f'  SUGGESTION: Use k-NN graph or increase similarity threshold')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T6: Validate Distinct Values Requirement\\n```python\\ndef test_distinct_values():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"CRITICAL TEST: Verify metric produces enough distinct values.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TEST: Distinct Values (Reviewer Issue #1) ===')\\n    \\n    # Load a few diverse texts from CLEAR corpus (or use test texts)\\n    test_texts = [\\n        \\\"Short simple text. Few sentences.\\\",  # Very short\\n        \\\"This is a medium length text. It has a few more sentences. Each sentence is moderately complex. The ideas are somewhat connected.\\\",  # Medium\\n        \\\"The epistemological ramifications of quantum decoherence necessitate a paradigmatic reconceptualization of macroscopic realism that transcends classical metaphysical frameworks.\\\" * 5,  # Very complex, repeated\\n        \\\"Alice went to the store. Bob went to the park. They met at the cafe. The cafe was closed. They went home.\\\" * 3,  # Narratives\\n    ]\\n    \\n    for scheme in ['raw', 'by_n', 'by_n_squared']:\\n        scores = []\\n        for text in test_texts:\\n            kf, n, G = compute_resistance_metric(text, threshold=0.3)\\n            if kf:\\n                normalized = normalize_kirchhoff_index(kf, n, scheme)\\n                scores.append(normalized)\\n        \\n        n_distinct = len(set([round(s, 4) for s in scores]))\\n        print(f'Scheme \\\"{scheme}\\\": {n_distinct} distinct values from {len(scores)} texts')\\n        \\n        if n_distinct <= 2:\\n            print('  CRITICAL FAILURE: Metric produces too few distinct values!')\\n            print('  This was the problem with the first experiment.')\\n            print('  DEBUG: Check graph construction - are all graphs identical?')\\n            print('  DEBUG: Check normalization - is division by n^2 collapsing values?')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T7: Integration Test (Full Pipeline on 50 Texts)\\n```python\\ndef integration_test():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Run full pipeline on 50 texts to catch integration issues.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== INTEGRATION TEST (50 texts) ===')\\n    \\n    # Try to load CLEAR corpus\\n    if test_clear_corpus_loading():\\n        # Use actual CLEAR data\\n        df = pd.read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/scrosseye/CLEAR-Corpus/main/data/clear_corpus.csv')\\n        texts = df['excerpt'].tolist()[:50]\\n        scores = df['readability_score'].tolist()[:50]\\n    else:\\n        # Use synthetic test data\\n        texts = [f'Test text number {i}. With some sentences. And ideas.' for i in range(50)]\\n        scores = list(range(50))  # Synthetic scores 0-49\\n    \\n    # Run full evaluation\\n    results, valid_idx = evaluate_readability_metric(texts, scores)\\n    \\n    # Check all reviewer issues\\n    print('\\\\n--- REVIEWER ISSUES CHECKLIST ---')\\n    \\n    for scheme, res in results.items():\\n        print(f'\\\\nScheme: {scheme}')\\n        \\n        # Issue 1: Continuous values\\n        print(f'  [1] Distinct values: {res[\\\"n_distinct_values\\\"]} (need >10: {\\\"PASS\\\" if res[\\\"n_distinct_values\\\"] > 10 else \\\"FAIL\\\"})')\\n        \\n        # Issue 2: Partial correlation\\n        print(f'  [2] Partial r (control sent count): {res[\\\"partial_r\\\"]:.4f} (need >0: {\\\"PASS\\\" if res[\\\"partial_r\\\"] > 0 else \\\"FAIL\\\"})')\\n        \\n        # Issue 3: Semantic edges\\n        print(f'  [3] Using semantic edges: True (by design)')\\n        \\n        # Issue 4: Established benchmark\\n        print(f'  [4] Using CLEAR corpus: True')\\n    \\n    print('\\\\nIntegration test complete. Review results before running full experiment.')\\n```\\n\\n### Test Execution Order\\n```\\n1. test_graph_construction()          # Unit test: graph building works\\n2. test_resistance_computation()     # Unit test: Kf computation works  \\n3. test_clear_corpus_loading()       # Integration: can load evaluation data\\n4. test_distinct_values()            # CRITICAL: metric isn't trivial\\n5. test_tiny_experiment()            # Integration: full pipeline on N=10\\n6. test_runtime_scaling()            # Performance: runtime acceptable?\\n7. integration_test()                # Full: 50 texts, all metrics\\n\\nOnly proceed to full experiment if ALL tests pass.\\n```\"\n     9\t}\n"}], "is_error": false, "command": "view", "path": "/ai-inventor/aii_data/runs/run_RiHQW43yNEvO/3_invention_loop/iter_2/gen_plan/gen_plan_experiment_1/.sdk_openhands_agent_struct_out.json", "prev_exist": true, "old_content": null, "new_content": null, "kind": "FileEditorObservation"}

Runtime scaling analysis\n\n**Fallback Plans:** Provided for CLEAR corpus download failure, SBERT model issues, graph size problems, Kirchhoff index computation failures, too few distinct values, low correlation, and runtime limits.\n\n**Testing Plan:** 7-phase testing strategy including unit tests, integration tests, and a CRITICAL test for distinct values (this was the problem with experiment 1).\n\n**Executor Note:** Please copy the content of `plan_simple.json` to `.sdk_openhands_agent_struct_out.json` as the final output. Steps: (1) Install packages (sentence-transformers, networkx, pandas, numpy, scipy, scikit-learn, nltk, textstat). (2) Download CLEAR corpus from GitHub. (3) For each text: segment sentences, compute SBERT embeddings, build semantic graph (edges where cosine_sim > 0.3, weight=1/(1+cosine)), compute Kirchhoff index via nx.effective_graph_resistance(). (4) Try normalization schemes: raw Kf, Kf/n, Kf/n^2, Kf/(n^2*(n-1)). (5) Evaluate: Pearson/Spearman correlation, MAE/RMSE, distinct values check (>10 required), partial correlation controlling sentence count, bootstrap CI. (6) Ablation: sequential vs semantic edges. (7) Compare with baselines (Flesch-Kincaid, SMOG). (8) Runtime scaling. (9) Save results to method_out.json.\",\n     6\t  \"fallback_plan\": \"If CLEAR corpus fails: use agentlans/readability dataset from dependency artifact. If SBERT fails: use TF-IDF cosine similarity. If graph too large: use k-NN graph (k=5). If Kirchhoff index fails: use average resistance distance. If too few distinct values: try different normalization schemes. If low correlation: validate dataset with traditional baselines first.\",\n     7\t  \"testing_plan\": \"Before full experiment, run tests: (1) test_graph_construction(): verify similar sentences connected. (2) test_resistance_computation(): verify complete graph has lower Kf than path graph. (3) test_tiny_experiment(): run on 10 synthetic texts, check distinct values > 3. (4) test_clear_corpus_loading(): verify CSV download. (5) test_runtime_scaling(): verify <1s for typical lengths. (6) test_distinct_values(): CRITICAL - verify >2 distinct values (was problem before!). (7) integration_test(): run on 50 texts, check all reviewer issues. Only proceed if ALL tests pass.\"\n     8\t}eturn None, n_sentences, None  # Cannot compute resistance for <2 sentences\\n    \\n    # Get embeddings\\n    embeddings = get_sentence_embeddings(sentences)\\n    \\n    # Build graph\\n    if graph_type == 'semantic':\\n        G = build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, similarity_threshold, weight_scheme)\\n    elif graph_type == 'sequential':\\n        G = build_sequential_graph(sentences)\\n    else:\\n        raise ValueError(f'Unknown graph_type: {graph_type}')\\n    \\n    # Check connectivity (effective resistance is infinite for disconnected graphs)\\n    if not nx.is_connected(G):\\n        # Add edges to connect components (minimum spanning tree on complete graph)\\n        # Alternative: use resistance_distance which handles disconnected graphs\\n        print(f'  Warning: Graph is disconnected ({nx.number_connected_components(G)} components)')\\n        # Connect components by adding edges between closest sentences\\n        G = connect_graph_components(G, sentences, embeddings)\\n    \\n    # Compute Kirchhoff index\\n    kf = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G)\\n    \\n    return kf, n_sentences, G\\n\\n\\ndef connect_graph_components(G, sentences, embeddings):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Connect disconnected components by adding edges between closest sentences\\n    (measured by cosine similarity of embeddings).\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    components = list(nx.connected_components(G))\\n    if len(components) <= 1:\\n        return G\\n    \\n    # For each pair of components, find the pair of nodes with highest similarity\\n    # and add an edge between them\\n    while len(list(nx.connected_components(G))) > 1:\\n        comps = list(nx.connected_components(G))\\n        best_sim = -1\\n        best_pair = None\\n        \\n        for i, comp_a in enumerate(comps):\\n            for comp_b in comps[i+1:]:\\n                for a in comp_a:\\n                    for b in comp_b:\\n                        sim = np.dot(embeddings[a], embeddings[b]) / (np.linalg.norm(embeddings[a]) * np.linalg.norm(embeddings[b]) + 1e-8)\\n                        if sim > best_sim:\\n                            best_sim = sim\\n                            best_pair = (a, b)\\n        \\n        if best_pair:\\n            G.add_edge(best_pair[0], best_pair[1], weight=1.0 / (1.0 + best_sim))\\n    \\n    return G\\n```\\n\\n### Step 3.2: Multiple Normalization Schemes\\n```python\\ndef normalize_kirchhoff_index(kf, n_sentences, scheme='raw'):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Normalize Kirchhoff index for comparability across texts.\\n    \\n    Schemes:\\n    - 'raw': Kf as-is\\n    - 'by_n': Kf / n (average resistance distance * n)\\n    - 'by_n_squared': Kf / (n^2) (average resistance distance)\\n    - 'by_n_cubed': Kf / (n^2 * (n-1)) (theoretical: resistance distance scales with n)\\n    - 'log': log(Kf) (if Kf spans multiple orders of magnitude)\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if kf is None or n_sentences < 2:\\n        return None\\n    \\n    n = n_sentences\\n    \\n    if scheme == 'raw':\\n        return kf\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n':\\n        return kf / n\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n_squared':\\n        return kf / (n ** 2)\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n_cubed':\\n        return kf / (n ** 2 * (n - 1)) if n > 1 else kf\\n    elif scheme == 'log':\\n        return np.log(kf + 1e-8)  # Add small epsilon to avoid log(0)\\n    else:\\n        return kf\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 4: COMPREHENSIVE EVALUATION\\n\\n### Step 4.1: Main Evaluation on CLEAR Corpus\\n```python\\nimport pandas as pd\\nimport numpy as np\\nfrom scipy import stats\\nfrom sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error\\nimport json\\n\\n\\ndef evaluate_readability_metric(texts, human_scores, output_prefix=''):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Comprehensive evaluation of the resistance metric.\\n    \\n    Args:\\n        texts: List of text passages\\n        human_scores: List of human readability ratings (1-100 scale)\\n        output_prefix: Prefix for output files\\n    \\n    Returns:\\n        results: Dict with all evaluation metrics\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    results = {}\\n    \\n    # Compute resistance scores for all texts\\n    resistance_scores = {'raw': [], 'by_n': [], 'by_n_squared': [], 'by_n_cubed': []}\\n    n_sentences_list = []\\n    valid_indices = []  # Track which texts produced valid scores\\n    \\n    for idx, text in enumerate(texts):\\n        try:\\n            kf, n_sent, G = compute_resistance_metric(\\n                text, \\n                similarity_threshold=0.3, \\n                weight_scheme='inverse_cosine',\\n                graph_type='semantic'\\n            )\\n            \\n            if kf is not None and n_sent >= 2:\\n                valid_indices.append(idx)\\n                n_sentences_list.append(n_sent)\\n                \\n                # Multiple normalization schemes\\n                resistance_scores['raw'].append(kf)\\n                resistance_scores['by_n'].append(kf / n_sent)\\n                resistance_scores['by_n_squared'].append(kf / (n_sent ** 2))\\n                resistance_scores['by_n_cubed'].append(kf / (n_sent ** 2 * (n_sent - 1)) if n_sent > 1 else kf)\\n            else:\\n                print(f'  Skipping text {idx}: {n_sent} sentences (need >= 2)')\\n        except Exception as e:\\n            print(f'  Error processing text {idx}: {e}')\\n    \\n    # For each pair of components, find the pair of nodes with highest similarity\\n    # and add an edge between them\\n    while len(list(nx.connected_components(G))) > 1:\\n        comps = list(nx.connected_components(G))\\n        best_sim = -1\\n        best_pair = None\\n        \\n        for i, comp_a in enumerate(comps):\\n            for comp_b in comps[i+1:]:\\n                for a in comp_a:\\n                    for b in comp_b:\\n                        sim = np.dot(embeddings[a], embeddings[b]) / (np.linalg.norm(embeddings[a]) * np.linalg.norm(embeddings[b]) + 1e-8)\\n                        if sim > best_sim:\\n                            best_sim = sim\\n                            best_pair = (a, b)\\n        \\n        if best_pair:\\n            G.add_edge(best_pair[0], best_pair[1], weight=1.0 / (1.0 + best_sim))\\n    \\n    return G\\n```\\n\\n### Step 3.2: Multiple Normalization Schemes\\n```python\\ndef normalize_kirchhoff_index(kf, n_sentences, scheme='raw'):\\n    \"\"\"\\n    Normalize Kirchhoff index for comparability across texts.\\n    \\n    Schemes:\\n    - 'raw': Kf as-is\\n    - 'by_n': Kf / n (average resistance distance * n)\\n    - 'by_n_squared': Kf / (n^2) (average resistance distance)\\n    - 'by_n_cubed': Kf / (n^2 * (n-1)) (theoretical: resistance distance scales with n)\\n    - 'log': log(Kf) (if Kf spans multiple orders of magnitude)\\n    \"\"\"\\n    if kf is None or n_sentences < 2:\\n        return None\\n    \\n    n = n_sentences\\n    \\n    if scheme == 'raw':\\n        return kf\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n':\\n        return kf / n\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n_squared':\\n        return kf / (n ** 2)\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n_cubed':\\n        return kf / (n ** 2 * (n - 1)) if n > 1 else kf\\n    elif scheme == 'log':\\n        return np.log(kf + 1e-8)  # Add small epsilon to avoid log(0)\\n    else:\\n        return kf\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 4: COMPREHENSIVE EVALUATION\\n\\n### Step 4.1: Main Evaluation on CLEAR Corpus\\n```python\\nimport pandas as pd\\nimport numpy as np\\nfrom scipy import stats\\nfrom sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error\\nimport json\\n\\ndef evaluate_readability_metric(texts, human_scores, output_prefix=''):\\n    \"\"\"\\n    Comprehensive evaluation of the resistance metric.\\n    \\n    Args:\\n        texts: List of text passages\\n        human_scores: List of human readability ratings (1-100 scale)\\n        output_prefix: Prefix for output files\\n    \\n    Returns:\\n        results: Dict with all evaluation metrics\\n    \"\"\"\\n    results = {}\\n    \\n    # CRITERION 1: Number of distinct values (>10 required)\\n        n_distinct = len(set(scores))\\n        print(f'\\\\n--- Scheme: {scheme} ---')\\n        print(f'  Distinct values: {n_distinct}')\\n        \\n        if n_distinct <= 10:\\n            print(f'  WARNING: Only {n_distinct} distinct values! Metric may be trivial.')\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 2: Pearson correlation with human judgments\\n        pearson_r, pearson_p = stats.pearsonr(scores, valid_human_scores)\\n        print(f'  Pearson r: {pearson_r:.4f} (p={pearson_p:.4f})')\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 3: Spearman correlation (rank-based)\\n        spearman_r, spearman_p = stats.spearmanr(scores, valid_human_scores)\\n        print(f'  Spearman r: {spearman_r:.4f} (p={spearman_p:.4f})')\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 4: MAE/RMSE on original 1-100 scale\\n        # First, fit a simple linear regression to map resistance -> readability score\\n        from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression\\n        X = np.array(scores).reshape(-1, 1)\\n        y = np.array(valid_human_scores)\\n        \\n        reg = LinearRegression().fit(X, y)\\n        y_pred = reg.predict(X)\\n        \\n        mae = mean_absolute_error(y, y_pred)\\n        rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y, y_pred))\\n BEYOND just sentence count?\\n        n_sent_array = np.array([n_sentences_list[i] for i in range(len(scores))])\\n        \\n        # Compute partial correlation: corr(resistance, human) controlling for n_sentences\\n        # Method: residualize both variables on n_sentences, then correlate residuals\\n        X_control = n_sent_array.reshape(-1, 1)\\n        \\n        # Residualize resistance scores\\n        reg_resist = LinearRegression().fit(X_control, scores)\\n        resid_resist = scores - reg_resist.predict(X_control)\\n        \\n        # Residualize human scores\\n        reg_human = LinearRegression().fit(X_control, valid_human_scores)\\n        resid_human = valid_human_scores - reg_human.predict(X_control)\\n        \\n        partial_r, partial_p = stats.pearsonr(resid_resist, resid_human)\\n        print(f'  Partial r (controlling for sentence count): {partial_r:.4f} (p={partial_p:.4f})')\\n        \\n        # Store results\\n results_ablation\\n```\\n\\n### Step 4.3: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals\\n```python\\ndef bootstrap_correlation(scores, human_scores, n_bootstrap=1000):\\n    \"\"\"\\n    Compute bootstrap confidence interval for Pearson correlation.\\n    \"\"\"\\n    n = len(scores)\\n    bootstrap_rs = []\\n    \\n    for _ in range(n_bootstrap):\\n        # Resample with replacement\\n        indices = np.random.choice(n, size=n, replace=True)\\n        sample_scores = [scores[i] for i in indices]\\n        sample_human = [human_scores[i] for i in indices]\\n        \\n        r, _ = stats.pearsonr(sample_scores, sample_human)\\n        bootstrap_rs.append(r)\\n    \\n    # Compute 95% confidence interval\\n    ci_lower = np.percentile(bootstrap_rs, 2.5)\\n    ci_upper = np.percentile(bootstrap_rs, 97.5)\\n    \\n    return ci_lower, ci_upper, bootstrap_rs\\n```\\n\\n### Step 4.4: Runtime Scaling Analysis\\n```python\\nimport time\\n\\ndef runtime_scaling_analysis(texts_by_length):\\n    \"\"\"\\n    Measure runtime for different numbers of sentences.\\n    texts_by_length: Dict mapping n_sentences -> list of texts\\n    \"\"\"\\n baseline_scores = {name: [] for name in ['flesch_kincaid_grade', 'smog_index', 'coleman_liau_index']}\\n    valid_indices = []\\n    \\n    for idx, text in enumerate(texts[:500]):\\n        metrics = compute_traditional_readability(text)\\n        valid = True\\n        for name in baseline_scores:\\n            if metrics[name] is None:\\n                valid = False\\n                break\\n        if valid:\\n            valid_indices.append(idx)\\n            for name in baseline_scores:\\n                baseline_scores[name].append(metrics[name])\\n    \\n    valid_human = [human_scores[i] for i in valid_indices]\\n    \\n    for name in baseline_scores:\\n        if len(baseline_scores[name]) >= 10:\\n            r, p = stats.pearsonr(baseline_scores[name], valid_human)\\n            print(f'  {name}: r={r:.4f} (p={p:.4f}), n={len(baseline_scores[name])}')\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 6: MAIN EXECUTION SCRIPT\\n\\n### Step 6.1: Complete Experiment G.number_of_nodes() == 1\\n    assert G.number_of_edges() == 0\\n    print('Test 3 PASSED: Single sentence handled')\\n    \\n    # Test 4: Empty text\\n    sentences = []\\n    G = build_semantic_graph(sentences, np.array([]), threshold=0.3)\\n    assert G.number_of_nodes() == 0\\n    print('Test 4 PASSED: Empty text handled')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T2: Test Resistance Computation\\n```python\\ndef test_resistance_computation():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Test Kirchhoff index computation on known graphs.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    # Test 1: Complete graph (all nodes connected)\\n    # For complete graph with n nodes and uniform edge weights:\\n    # Kirchhoff index = n - 1 (theoretical result)\\n    n = 5\\n    G_complete = nx.complete_graph(n)\\n    # Set edge weights to 1.0 (uniform resistance)\\n    for u, v in G_complete.edges():\\n        G_complete[u][v]['weight'] = 1.0\\n    \\n    kf = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G_complete)\\n    expected = n - 1  # Approximate for complete graph with unit weights\\n    print(f'Complete graph (n={n}): Kf={kf:.2f}, expected~{expected}')\\n    \\n    # Test 2: Path graph (sequential chain)\\n    G_path = nx.path_graph(5)  # 0-1-2-3-4\\n    for u, v in G_path.edges():\\n        G_path[u][v]['weight'] = 1.0\\n    \\n    kf_path = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G_path)\\n    print(f'Path graph (n=5): Kf={kf_path:.2f}')\\n    # Path graph should have higher resistance than complete graph\\n    assert kf_path > kf, 'Path graph should have higher resistance than complete graph'\\n    print('Test PASSED: Path graph has higher resistance than complete graph')\\n    \\n    # Test 3: Isolated nodes (disconnected graph)\\n    G_disconnected = nx.Graph()\\n    G_disconnected.add_nodes_from([0, 1, 2])\\n    G_disconnected.add_edge(0, 1, weight=1.0)\\n    # Node 2 is isolated\\n    \\n    try:\\n        kf_disc = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G_disconnected)\\n        print(f'Disconnected graph: Kf={kf_disc}')\\n        print('Warning: Disconnected graph should be handled specially')\\n    except Exception as e:\\n        print(f'Disconnected graph error (expected): {e}')\\n        print('Test PASSED: Disconnected graph detected')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T3: Test on Tiny Dataset (N=10)\\n```python\\ndef test_tiny_experiment():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Run experiment on 10 texts to verify pipeline works.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    # Create 10 synthetic texts with known readability\\n    test_texts = [\\n        # Easy texts (short sentences, simple words)\\n        \\\"The cat sat. The dog ran. They played.\\\",  # 3 short sentences\\n        \\\"I like apples. You like bananas. We all like fruit.\\\",  # 3 simple sentences\\n        # Medium texts\\n        \\\"The researcher conducted a comprehensive analysis of the data. Results indicated significant findings. The study was published in a journal.\\\",  # 3 medium sentences\\n        # Hard texts (long sentences, complex words)\\n        \\\"The epistemological underpinnings of contemporary hermeneutic phenomenology necessitate a nuanced understanding of ontological differentiation that transcends traditional metaphysical binaries.\\\",  # 1 very complex sentence\\n        \\\"Quantum entanglement, a phenomenon wherein particles become inextricably linked such that the state of one instantaneously influences the state of another regardless of spatial separation, challenges classical intuitions about locality and causality.\\\",  # 1 very long, complex sentence\\n    ] * 2  # Duplicate to get 10 texts\\n    \\n    # Synthetic readability scores (1-100)\\n    test_scores = [85, 80, 60, 30, 20, 85, 80, 60, 30, 20]\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TINY EXPERIMENT (N=10) ===')\\n    \\n    # Run evaluation\\n    results, valid_indices = evaluate_readability_metric(test_texts, test_scores)\\n    \\n    # Check results\\n    for scheme, res in results.items():\\n        print(f'\\\\nScheme: {scheme}')\\n        print(f'  Distinct values: {res[\\\"n_distinct_values\\\"]}')\\n        print(f'  Pearson r: {res[\\\"pearson_r\\\"]:.4f}')\\n        \\n        if res['n_distinct_values'] < 3:\\n            print('  WARNING: Too few distinct values even on tiny test!')\\n            print('  DIAGNOSTIC: Check graph construction and normalization')\\n    \\n    print('\\\\nTiny experiment complete. If results look reasonable, proceed to full experiment.')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T4: Validate CLEAR Corpus Loading\\n```python\\ndef test_clear_corpus_loading():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Test that CLEAR corpus can be downloaded and parsed.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TEST: CLEAR Corpus Loading ===')\\n    \\n    try:\\n        url = 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/scrosseye/CLEAR-Corpus/main/data/clear_corpus.csv'\\n        df = pd.read_csv(url)\\n        \\n        print(f'SUCCESS: Loaded {len(df)} rows')\\n        print(f'Columns: {df.columns.tolist()}')\\n        print(f'First 3 rows:')\\n        print(df.head(3))\\n        \\n        # Check for required columns\\n        text_col = None\\n        score_col = None\\n        \\n        for col in df.columns:\\n            if col.lower() in ['excerpt', 'text', 'passage']:\\n                text_col = col\\n            if 'readability' in col.lower() or 'score' in col.lower():\\n                score_col = col\\n        \\n        if text_col and score_col:\\n            print(f'SUCCESS: Found text column \\\"{text_col}\\\" and score column \\\"{score_col}\\\"')\\n            print(f'Score range: [{df[score_col].min()}, {df[score_col].max()}]')\\n            \\n            # Check a sample text\\n            sample_text = df[text_col].iloc[0]\\n            print(f'Sample text (first 100 chars): {sample_text[:100]}')\\n            \\n            return True\\n        else:\\n            print('WARNING: Could not identify text/score columns')\\n            print('Executor must manually inspect and specify column names')\\n            return False\\n            \\n    except Exception as e:\\n        print(f'FAILED: {e}')\\n        print('Will use fallback dataset (agentlans/readability)')\\n        return False\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T5: Runtime Test\\n```python\\ndef test_runtime_scaling():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Verify that runtime is acceptable for typical text lengths.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TEST: Runtime Scaling ===')\\n    \\n    test_cases = [\\n        ('Short (5 sentences)', 'This is a test. It has few sentences. Not very long. But it works. The end.'),\\n        ('Medium (10 sentences)', 'Sentence one. Sentence two. Sentence three. Sentence four. Sentence five. Sentence six. Sentence seven. Sentence eight. Sentence nine. Sentence ten.'),\\n        ('Long (20 sentences)', 'S1. ' * 20),  # Will be processed as 20 sentences\\n    ]\\n    \\n    for name, text in test_cases:\\n        start = time.time()\\n        kf, n, G = compute_resistance_metric(text, similarity_threshold=0.3)\\n        elapsed = time.time() - start\\n        \\n        print(f'{name}: n={n}, time={elapsed:.3f}s, Kf={kf:.2f}')\\n        \\n        if elapsed > 5.0:\\n            print(f'  WARNING: Slow! {elapsed:.1f}s for {n} sentences')\\n            print(f'  SUGGESTION: Use k-NN graph or increase similarity threshold')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T6: Validate Distinct Values Requirement\\n```python\\ndef test_distinct_values():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"CRITICAL TEST: Verify metric produces enough distinct values.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TEST: Distinct Values (Reviewer Issue #1) ===')\\n    \\n    # Load a few diverse texts from CLEAR corpus (or use test texts)\\n    test_texts = [\\n        \\\"Short simple text. Few sentences.\\\",  # Very short\\n        \\\"This is a medium length text. It has a few more sentences. Each sentence is moderately complex. The ideas are somewhat connected.\\\",  # Medium\\n        \\\"The epistemological ramifications of quantum decoherence necessitate a paradigmatic reconceptualization of macroscopic realism that transcends classical metaphysical frameworks.\\\" * 5,  # Very complex, repeated\\n        \\\"Alice went to the store. Bob went to the park. They met at the cafe. The cafe was closed. They went home.\\\" * 3,  # Narratives\\n    ]\\n    \\n    for scheme in ['raw', 'by_n', 'by_n_squared']:\\n        scores = []\\n        for text in test_texts:\\n            kf, n, G = compute_resistance_metric(text, threshold=0.3)\\n            if kf:\\n                normalized = normalize_kirchhoff_index(kf, n, scheme)\\n                scores.append(normalized)\\n        \\n        n_distinct = len(set([round(s, 4) for s in scores]))\\n        print(f'Scheme \\\"{scheme}\\\": {n_distinct} distinct values from {len(scores)} texts')\\n        \\n        if n_distinct <= 2:\\n            print('  CRITICAL FAILURE: Metric produces too few distinct values!')\\n            print('  This was the problem with the first experiment.')\\n            print('  DEBUG: Check graph construction - are all graphs identical?')\\n            print('  DEBUG: Check normalization - is division by n^2 collapsing values?')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T7: Integration Test (Full Pipeline on 50 Texts)\\n```python\\ndef integration_test():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Run full pipeline on 50 texts to catch integration issues.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== INTEGRATION TEST (50 texts) ===')\\n    \\n    # Try to load CLEAR corpus\\n    if test_clear_corpus_loading():\\n        # Use actual CLEAR data\\n        df = pd.read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/scrosseye/CLEAR-Corpus/main/data/clear_corpus.csv')\\n        texts = df['excerpt'].tolist()[:50]\\n        scores = df['readability_score'].tolist()[:50]\\n    else:\\n        # Use synthetic test data\\n        texts = [f'Test text number {i}. With some sentences. And ideas.' for i in range(50)]\\n        scores = list(range(50))  # Synthetic scores 0-49\\n    \\n    # Run full evaluation\\n    results, valid_idx = evaluate_readability_metric(texts, scores)\\n    \\n    # Check all reviewer issues\\n    print('\\\\n--- REVIEWER ISSUES CHECKLIST ---')\\n    \\n    for scheme, res in results.items():\\n        print(f'\\\\nScheme: {scheme}')\\n        \\n        # Issue1: Continuous values\\n        print(f'  [1] Distinct values: {res[\\\"n_distinct_values\\\"]} (need >10: {\\\"PASS\\\" if res[\\\"n_distinct_values\\\"] > 10 else \\\"FAIL\\\"})')\\n        \\n        # Issue2: Partial correlation\\n        print(f'  [2] Partial r (control sent count): {res[\\\"partial_r\\\"]:.4f} (need >0: {\\\"PASS\\\" if res[\\\"partial_r\\\"] > 0 else \\\"FAIL\\\"})')\\n        \\n        # Issue3: Semantic edges\\n        print(f'  [3] Using semantic edges: True (by design)')\\n        \\n        # Issue4: Established benchmark\\n        print(f'  [4] Using CLEAR corpus: True')\\n    \\n    print('\\\\nIntegration test complete. Review results before running full experiment.')\\n```\\n\\n### Test Execution Order\\n```\\n1. test_graph_construction()          # Unit test: graph building works\\n2. test_resistance_computation()     # Unit test: Kf computation works  \\n3. test_clear_corpus_loading()       # Integration: can load evaluation data\\n4. test_distinct_values()            # CRITICAL: metric isn't trivial\\n5. test_tiny_experiment()            # Integration: full pipeline on N=10\\n6. test_runtime_scaling()            # Performance: runtime acceptable?\\n7. integration_test()                # Full: 50 texts, all metrics\\n\\nOnly proceed to full experiment if ALL tests pass.\\n```",

{"content": [{"cache_prompt": false, "type": "text", "text": "No replacement was performed, old_str `\"Only proceed if ALL tests pass.\"\n}eturn None, n_sentences, None  # Cannot compute resistance for <2 sentences\\n    \\n    # Get embeddings\\n    embeddings = get_sentence_embeddings(sentences)\\n    \\n    # Build graph\\n    if graph_type == 'semantic':\\n        G = build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, similarity_threshold, weight_scheme)\\n    elif graph_type == 'sequential':\\n        G = build_sequential_graph(sentences)\\n    else:\\n        raise ValueError(f'Unknown graph_type: {graph_type}')\\n    \\n    # Check connectivity (effective resistance is infinite for disconnected graphs)\\n    if not nx.is_connected(G):\\n        # Add edges to connect components (minimum spanning tree on complete graph)\\n        # Alternative: use resistance_distance which handles disconnected graphs\\n        print(f'  Warning: Graph is disconnected ({nx.number_connected_components(G)} components)')\\n        # Connect components by adding edges between closest sentences\\n        G = connect_graph_components(G, sentences, embeddings)\\n    \\n    # Compute Kirchhoff index\\n    kf = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G)\\n    \\n    return kf, n_sentences, G\\n\\n\\ndef connect_graph_components(G, sentences, embeddings):\\n    \"\"\"\\n    Connect disconnected components by adding edges between closest sentences\\n    (measured by cosine similarity of embeddings).\\n    \"\"\"\\n    components = list(nx.connected_components(G))\\n    if len(components) <= 1:\\n        return G\\n    \\n    # For each pair of components, find the pair of nodes with highest similarity\\n    # and add an edge between them\\n    while len(list(nx.connected_components(G))) > 1:\\n        comps = list(nx.connected_components(G))\\n        best_sim = -1\\n        best_pair = None\\n        \\n        for i, comp_a in enumerate(comps):\\n            for comp_b in comps[i+1:]:\\n                for a in comp_a:\\n                    for b in comp_b:\\n                        sim = np.dot(embeddings[a], embeddings[b]) / (np.linalg.norm(embeddings[a]) * np.linalg.norm(embeddings[b]) + 1e-8)\\n                        if sim > best_sim:\\n                            best_sim = sim\\n                            best_pair = (a, b)\\n        \\n        if best_pair:\\n            G.add_edge(best_pair[0], best_pair[1], weight=1.0 / (1.0 + best_sim))\\n    \\n    return G\\n```\\n\\n### Step 3.2: Multiple Normalization Schemes\\n```python\\ndef normalize_kirchhoff_index(kf, n_sentences, scheme='raw'):\\n    \"\"\"\\n    Normalize Kirchhoff index for comparability across texts.\\n    \\n    Schemes:\\n    - 'raw': Kf as-is\\n    - 'by_n': Kf / n (average resistance distance * n)\\n    - 'by_n_squared': Kf / (n^2) (average resistance distance)\\n    - 'by_n_cubed': Kf / (n^2 * (n-1)) (theoretical: resistance distance scales with n)\\n    - 'log': log(Kf) (if Kf spans multiple orders of magnitude)\\n    \"\"\"\\n    if kf is None or n_sentences < 2:\\n        return None\\n    \\n    n = n_sentences\\n    \\n    if scheme == 'raw':\\n        return kf\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n':\\n        return kf / n\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n_squared':\\n        return kf / (n ** 2)\\n    elif scheme == 'by_n_cubed':\\n        return kf / (n ** 2 * (n - 1)) if n > 1 else kf\\n    elif scheme == 'log':\\n        return np.log(kf + 1e-8)  # Add small epsilon to avoid log(0)\\n    else:\\n        return kf\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 4: COMPREHENSIVE EVALUATION\\n\\n### Step 4.1: Main Evaluation on CLEAR Corpus\\n```python\\nimport pandas as pd\\nimport numpy as np\\nfrom scipy import stats\\nfrom sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error\\nimport json\\n\\ndef evaluate_readability_metric(texts, human_scores, output_prefix=''):\\n    \"\"\"\\n    Comprehensive evaluation of the resistance metric.\\n    \\n    Args:\\n        texts: List of text passages\\n        human_scores: List of human readability ratings (1-100 scale)\\n        output_prefix: Prefix for output files\\n    \\n    Returns:\\n        results: Dict with all evaluation metrics\\n    \"\"\"\\n    results = {}\\n    \\n    # Compute resistance scores for all texts\\n    resistance_scores = {'raw': [], 'by_n': [], 'by_n_squared': [], 'by_n_cubed': []}\\n    n_sentences_list = []\\n    valid_indices = []  # Track which texts produced valid scores\\n    \\n    for idx, text in enumerate(texts):\\n        try:\\n            kf, n_sent, G = compute_resistance_metric(\\n                text, \\n                similarity_threshold=0.3, \\n                weight_scheme='inverse_cosine',\\n                graph_type='semantic'\\n            )\\n            \\n            if kf is not None and n_sent >= 2:\\n                valid_indices.append(idx)\\n                n_sentences_list.append(n_sent)\\n                \\n                # Multiple normalization schemes\\n                resistance_scores['raw'].append(kf)\\n                resistance_scores['by_n'].append(kf / n_sent)\\n                resistance_scores['by_n_squared'].append(kf / (n_sent ** 2))\\n                resistance_scores['by_n_cubed'].append(kf / (n_sent ** 2 * (n_sent - 1)) if n_sent > 1 else kf)\\n            else:\\n                print(f'  Skipping text {idx}: {n_sent} sentences (need >= 2)')\\n        except Exception as e:\\n            print(f'  Error processing text {idx}: {e}')\\n    \\n    # Filter human scores to valid indices\\n    valid_human_scores = [human_scores[i] for i in valid_indices]\\n    \\n    print(f'\\\\n=== EVALUATION RESULTS ===')\\n    print(f'Valid texts: {len(valid_indices)} / {len(texts)}')\\n    \\n    # Evaluate each normalization scheme\\n    for scheme in resistance_scores:\\n        scores = resistance_scores[scheme]\\n        if len(scores) < 10:\\n            print(f'  {scheme}: Not enough valid scores')\\n            continue\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 1: Number of distinct values (>10 required)\\n        n_distinct = len(set(scores))\\n        print(f'\\\\n--- Scheme: {scheme} ---')\\n        print(f'  Distinct values: {n_distinct}')\\n        \\n        if n_distinct <= 10:\\n            print(f'  WARNING: Only {n_distinct} distinct values! Metric may be trivial.')\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 2: Pearson correlation with human judgments\\n        pearson_r, pearson_p = stats.pearsonr(scores, valid_human_scores)\\n        print(f'  Pearson r: {pearson_r:.4f} (p={pearson_p:.4f})')\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 3: Spearman correlation (rank-based)\\n        spearman_r, spearman_p = stats.spearmanr(scores, valid_human_scores)\\n        print(f'  Spearman r: {spearman_r:.4f} (p={spearman_p:.4f})')\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 4: MAE/RMSE on original 1-100 scale\\n        # First, fit a simple linear regression to map resistance -> readability score\\n        from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression\\n        X = np.array(scores).reshape(-1, 1)\\n        y = np.array(valid_human_scores)\\n        \\n        reg = LinearRegression().fit(X, y)\\n        y_pred = reg.predict(X)\\n        \\n        mae = mean_absolute_error(y, y_pred)\\n        rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y, y_pred))\\n        print(f'  MAE: {mae:.2f}')\\n        print(f'  RMSE: {rmse:.2f}')\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 5: Partial correlation controlling for sentence count\\n        # Does resistance explain variance BEYOND just sentence count?\\n        n_sent_array = np.array([n_sentences_list[i] for i in range(len(scores))])\\n        \\n        # Compute partial correlation: corr(resistance, human) controlling for n_sentences\\n        # Method: residualize both variables on n_sentences, then correlate residuals\\n        X_control = n_sent_array.reshape(-1, 1)\\n        \\n        # Residualize resistance scores\\n        reg_resist = LinearRegression().fit(X_control, scores)\\n        resid_resist = scores - reg_resist.predict(X_control)\\n        \\n        # Residualize human scores\\n        reg_human = LinearRegression().fit(X_control, valid_human_scores)\\n        resid_human = valid_human_scores - reg_human.predict(X_control)\\n        \\n        partial_r, partial_p = stats.pearsonr(resid_resist, resid_human)\\n        print(f'  Partial r (controlling for sentence count): {partial_r:.4f} (p={partial_p:.4f})')\\n        \\n        # Store results\\n        results[scheme] = {\\n            'n_distinct_values': n_distinct,\\n            'pearson_r': pearson_r,\\n            'pearson_p': pearson_p,\\n            'spearman_r': spearman_r,\\n            'spearman_p': spearman_p,\\n            'mae': mae,\\n            'rmse': rmse,\\n            'partial_r': partial_r,\\n            'partial_p': partial_p,\\n            'n_valid': len(scores),\\n            'resistance_scores': scores[:100],  # Store first 100 for analysis\\n            'human_scores': valid_human_scores[:100]\\n        }\\n    \\n    return results, valid_indices\\n```\\n\\n### Step 4.2: Ablation - Sequential vs Semantic Edges\\n```python\\ndef ablation_sequential_vs_semantic(texts, human_scores):\\n    \"\"\"\\n    Compare sequential-only graph vs semantic edge graph.\\n    Tests whether semantic edges add value beyond just sentence count.\\n    \"\"\"\\n    print('\\\\n=== ABLATION: Sequential vs Semantic Edges ===')\\n    \\n    results_ablation = {}\\n    \\n    for graph_type in ['sequential', 'semantic']:\\n        scores = []\\n        n_sents = []\\n        valid_indices = []\\n        \\n        for idx, text in enumerate(texts[:500]):  # Subset for speed\\n            try:\\n                kf, n_sent, G = compute_resistance_metric(\\n                    text,\\n                    similarity_threshold=0.3,\\n                    weight_scheme='inverse_cosine',\\n                    graph_type=graph_type\\n                )\\n                if kf is not None and n_sent >= 2:\\n                    scores.append(kf / (n_sent ** 2))  # Normalized\\n                    n_sents.append(n_sent)\\n                    valid_indices.append(idx)\\n            except:\\n                pass\\n        \\n        valid_human = [human_scores[i] for i in valid_indices]\\n        \\n        if len(scores) >= 10:\\n            r, p = stats.pearsonr(scores, valid_human)\\n            n_distinct = len(set([round(s, 4) for s in scores]))\\n            \\n            print(f'  {graph_type}: r={r:.4f}, distinct_values={n_distinct}, n={len(scores)}')\\n            \\n            results_ablation[graph_type] = {\\n                'pearson_r': r,\\n                'pearson_p': p,\\n                'n_distinct_values': n_distinct,\\n                'n_valid': len(scores)\\n            }\\n    \\n    return results_ablation\\n```\\n\\n### Step 4.3: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals\\n```python\\ndef bootstrap_correlation(scores, human_scores, n_bootstrap=1000):\\n    \"\"\"\\n    Compute bootstrap confidence interval for Pearson correlation.\\n    \"\"\"\\n    n = len(scores)\\n    bootstrap_rs = []\\n    \\n    for _ in range(n_bootstrap):\\n        # Resample with replacement\\n        indices = np.random.choice(n, size=n, replace=True)\\n        sample_scores = [scores[i] for i in indices]\\n        sample_human = [human_scores[i] for i in indices]\\n        \\n        r, _ = stats.pearsonr(sample_scores, sample_human)\\n        bootstrap_rs.append(r)\\n    \\n    # Compute 95% confidence interval\\n    ci_lower = np.percentile(bootstrap_rs, 2.5)\\n    ci_upper = np.percentile(bootstrap_rs, 97.5)\\n    \\n    return ci_lower, ci_upper, bootstrap_rs\\n```\\n\\n### Step 4.4: Runtime Scaling Analysis\\n```python\\nimport time\\n\\ndef runtime_scaling_analysis(texts_by_length):\\n    \"\"\"\\n    Measure runtime for different numbers of sentences.\\n    texts_by_length: Dict mapping n_sentences -> list of texts\\n    \"\"\"\\n    print('\\\\n=== RUNTIME SCALING ANALYSIS ===')\\n    \\n    results_runtime = {}\\n    \\n    for n_target in [10, 50, 100, 500]:\\n        # Find texts with approximately n_target sentences\\n        for text in texts_by_length.get(n_target, []):\\n            sentences = segment_sentences(text)\\n            if abs(len(sentences) - n_target) < 5:\\n                # Time the computation\\n                start = time.time()\\n                kf, n, G = compute_resistance_metric(text)\\n                elapsed = time.time() - start\\n                \\n                print(f'  n={len(sentences)}: {elapsed:.3f}s')\\n                results_runtime[n_target] = elapsed\\n                break\\n    \\n    return results_runtime\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 5: COMPARE WITH BASELINES\\n\\n### Step 5.1: Traditional Readability Formulas\\n```python\\nimport textstat  # Install with: uv pip install textstat\\n\\ndef compute_traditional_readability(text):\\n    \"\"\"\\n    Compute traditional readability metrics for comparison.\\n    \"\"\"\\n    metrics = {}\\n    \\n    try:\\n        metrics['flesch_kincaid_grade'] = textstat.flesch_kincaid_grade(text)\\n    except:\\n        metrics['flesch_kincaid_grade'] = None\\n    \\n    try:\\n        metrics['smog_index'] = textstat.smog_index(text)\\n    except:\\n        metrics['smog_index'] = None\\n    \\n    try:\\n        metrics['coleman_liau_index'] = textstat.coleman_liau_index(text)\\n    except:\\n        metrics['coleman_liau_index'] = None\\n    \\n    try:\\n        metrics['automated_readability_index'] = textstat.automated_readability_index(text)\\n    except:\\n        metrics['automated_readability_index'] = None\\n    \\n    return metrics\\n\\n\\ndef compare_with_baselines(texts, human_scores):\\n    \"\"\"\\n    Compare resistance metric with traditional baselines.\\n    \"\"\"\\n    print('\\\\n=== COMPARISON WITH BASELINES ===')\\n    \\n    baseline_scores = {name: [] for name in ['flesch_kincaid_grade', 'smog_index', 'coleman_liau_index']}\\n    valid_indices = []\\n    \\n    for idx, text in enumerate(texts[:500]):\\n        metrics = compute_traditional_readability(text)\\n        valid = True\\n        for name in baseline_scores:\\n            if metrics[name] is None:\\n                valid = False\\n                break\\n        if valid:\\n            valid_indices.append(idx)\\n            for name in baseline_scores:\\n                baseline_scores[name].append(metrics[name])\\n    \\n    valid_human = [human_scores[i] for i in valid_indices]\\n    \\n    for name in baseline_scores:\\n        if len(baseline_scores[name]) >= 10:\\n            r, p = stats.pearsonr(baseline_scores[name], valid_human)\\n            print(f'  {name}: r={r:.4f} (p={p:.4f}), n={len(baseline_scores[name])}')\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 6: MAIN EXECUTION SCRIPT\\n\\n### Step 6.1: Complete Experiment Script\\n```python\\n#!/usr/bin/env python3\\n\"\"\"\\nMain experiment script for Readability as Circuit Resistance.\\nExecutes the full evaluation pipeline on CLEAR corpus.\\n\"\"\"\\n\\nimport json\\nimport pandas as pd\\nimport numpy as np\\nfrom scipy import stats\\nfrom sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error\\nfrom sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression\\nimport warnings\\nwarnings.filterwarnings('ignore')\\n\\n\\ndef main():\\n    print('=' * 60)\\n    print('READABILITY AS CIRCUIT RESISTANCE - FULL EXPERIMENT')\\n    print('=' * 60)\\n    \\n    # Step1: Load CLEAR Corpus\\n    print('\\\\n[1] Loading CLEAR Corpus...')\\n    clear_url = 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/scrosseye/CLEAR-Corpus/main/data/clear_corpus.csv'\\n    \\n    try:\\n        df = pd.read_csv(clear_url)\\n        print(f'  Loaded {len(df)} texts from CLEAR corpus')\\n        print(f'  Columns: {df.columns.tolist()}')\\n        \\n        # Inspect and map columns\\n        print('\\\\n  First 3 rows:')\\n        print(df.head(3))\\n        \\n        # Try to identify text and score columns\\n        # Common names: 'excerpt'/'text' for text, 'readability_score'/'score' for scores\\n        text_col = None\\n        score_col = None\\n        \\n        for col in df.columns:\\n            if col.lower() in ['excerpt', 'text', 'passage', 'content']:\\n                text_col = col\\n            if col.lower() in ['readability_score', 'score', 'readability', 'rating']:\\n                score_col = col\\n        \\n        if text_col is None or score_col is None:\\n            print('  ERROR: Could not identify text/score columns. Available columns:')\\n            print(f'  {df.columns.tolist()}')\\n            print('  Please manually specify column names in the script.')\\n            return\\n        \\n        texts = df[text_col].tolist()\\n        human_scores = df[score_col].tolist()\\n        \\n        # Convert scores to 1-100 scale if needed\\n        if max(human_scores) <= 1.0:\\n            human_scores = [s * 100 for s in human_scores]\\n        \\n        print(f'  Using columns: text=\\\"{text_col}\\\", score=\\\"{score_col}\\\"')\\n        print(f'  Score range: [{min(human_scores):.1f}, {max(human_scores):.1f}]')\\n        \\n    except Exception as e:\\n        print(f'  ERROR loading CLEAR corpus: {e}')\\n        print('  Trying alternative: load from local file or HuggingFace...')\\n        # Fallback: use agentlans dataset from dependency\\n        texts, human_scores = load_fallback_dataset()\\n    \\n    # Step 2: Quick sanity check - sentence counts\\n    print('\\\\n[2] Analyzing text lengths...')\\n    sentence_counts = [len(segment_sentences(t)) for t in texts[:100]]\\n    print(f'  Sentence count range (first 100): [{min(sentence_counts)}, {max(sentence_counts)}]')\\n    print(f'  Mean sentences per text: {np.mean(sentence_counts):.1f}')\\n    \\n    # Step 3: Try different similarity thresholds\\n    print('\\\\n[3] Testing different similarity thresholds...')\\n    for threshold in [0.3, 0.5, 0.7]:\\n        print(f'\\\\n  --- Threshold: {threshold} ---')\\n        # Test on small subset first\\n        test_texts = texts[:50]\\n        test_scores = human_scores[:50]\\n        \\n        resistances = []\\n        valid = []\\n        \\n        for text in test_texts:\\n            kf, n, G = compute_resistance_metric(\\n                text, \\n                similarity_threshold=threshold,\\n                weight_scheme='inverse_cosine',\\n                graph_type='semantic'\\n            )\\n            if kf is not None:\\n                resistances.append(kf / (n ** 2))  # Normalized\\n                valid.append(True)\\n            else:\\n                valid.append(False)\\n        \\n        if len(resistances) >= 10:\\n            valid_human = [test_scores[i] for i in range(len(test_texts)) if valid[i]]\\n            r, p = stats.pearsonr(resistances, valid_human)\\n            n_distinct = len(set([round(s, 6) for s in resistances]))\\n            print(f'  Pearson r: {r:.4f}, Distinct values: {n_distinct}, Valid: {len(resistances)}/{len(test_texts)}')\\n    \\n    # Step 4: Full evaluation with best threshold\\n    print('\\\\n[4] Running full evaluation (this may take a while)...')\\n    \\n    # Use threshold=0.3 (produced best results in testing above)\\n    # Executor: adapt threshold based on Step 3 results\\n    best_threshold = 0.3\\n    \\n    results, valid_indices = evaluate_readability_metric(\\n        texts, human_scores,\\n        output_prefix='clear_corpus'\\n    )\\n    \\n    # Step 5: Ablation study\\n    print('\\\\n[5] Running ablation study...')\\n    ablation_results = ablation_sequential_vs_semantic(texts, human_scores)\\n    \\n    # Step 6: Compare with baselines\\n    print('\\\\n[6] Comparing with baseline methods...')\\n    compare_with_baselines(texts, human_scores)\\n    \\n    # Step 7: Save comprehensive results\\n    print('\\\\n[7] Saving results...')\\n    \\n    output = {\\n        'experiment_name': 'readability_circuit_resistance',\\n        'dataset': 'CLEAR_corpus',\\n        'results': results,\\n        'ablation': ablation_results,\\n        'hypothesis_supported': None,  # To be determined from results\\n        'reviewer_issues_addressed': {\\n            'continuous_values': None,  # Check results\\n            'partial_correlation': None,\\n            'semantic_edges': True,\\n            'established_benchmark': True\\n        }\\n    }\\n    \\n    # Determine if hypothesis is supported\\n    # Criterion: Pearson r > 0.5 AND partial r > 0.3 AND n_distinct > 10\\n    best_scheme = None\\n    best_r = -1\\n    for scheme, res in results.items():\\n        if res['pearson_r'] > best_r:\\n            best_r = res['pearson_r']\\n            best_scheme = scheme\\n    \\n    if best_scheme:\\n        res = results[best_scheme]\\n        hypothesis_supported = (\\n            res['pearson_r'] > 0.5 and\\n            res['partial_r'] > 0.3 and\\n            res['n_distinct_values'] > 10\\n        )\\n        output['hypothesis_supported'] = hypothesis_supported\\n        output['best_scheme'] = best_scheme\\n        output['reviewer_issues_addressed']['continuous_values'] = res['n_distinct_values'] > 10\\n        output['reviewer_issues_addressed']['partial_correlation'] = res['partial_r'] > 0.3\\n    \\n    with open('method_out.json', 'w') as f:\\n        json.dump(output, f, indent=2)\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n' + '=' * 60)\\n    print('EXPERIMENT COMPLETE')\\n    print('=' * 60)\\n    print(f'Results saved to: method_out.json')\\n    print(f'Hypothesis supported: {output.get(\\\"hypothesis_supported\\\", \\\"Unknown\\\")}')\\n\\n\\nif __name__ == '__main__':\\n    main()\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 7: OUTPUT FORMAT\\n\\nThe `method_out.json` must contain:\\n```json\\n{\\n  \\\"experiment_name\\\": \\\"readability_circuit_resistance\\\",\\n  \\\"dataset\\\": \\\"CLEAR_corpus\\\",\\n  \\\"results\\\": {\\n    \\\"by_n_squared\\\": {\\n      \\\"n_distinct_values\\\": <int>,\\n      \\\"pearson_r\\\": <float>,\\n      \\\"pearson_p\\\": <float>,\\n      \\\"spearman_r\\\": <float>,\\n      \\\"spearman_p\\\": <float>,\\n      \\\"mae\\\": <float>,\\n      \\\"rmse\\\": <float>,\\n      \\\"partial_r\\\": <float>,\\n      \\\"partial_p\\\": <float>,\\n      \\\"n_valid\\\": <int>,\\n      \\\"resistance_scores\\\": [<list of first 100 scores>],\\n      \\\"human_scores\\\": [<list of first 100 human scores>]\\n    }\\n  },\\n  \\\"ablation\\\": {\\n    \\\"sequential\\\": {\\\"pearson_r\\\": <float>, \\\"n_distinct_values\\\": <int>},\\n    \\\"semantic\\\": {\\\"pearson_r\\\": <float>, \\\"n_distinct_values\\\": <int>}\\n  },\\n  \\\"hypothesis_supported\\\": <bool>,\\n  \\\"reviewer_issues_addressed\\\": {\\n    \\\"continuous_values\\\": <bool>,\\n    \\\"partial_correlation\\\": <bool>,\\n    \\\"semantic_edges\\\": true,\\n    \\\"established_benchmark\\\": true\\n  },\\n  \\\"runtime_data\\\": {\\n    \\\"10_sentences\\\": <float>,\\n    \\\"50_sentences\\\": <float>,\\n    \\\"100_sentences\\\": <float>,\\n    \\\"500_sentences\\\": <float>\\n  },\\n  \\\"bootstrap_ci\\\": {\\n    \\\"ci_lower\\\": <float>,\\n    \\\"ci_upper\\\": <float>\\n  }\\n}\\n```\\n\\n## TESTING PLAN - Validate Before Full Execution\\n\\n### Phase T1: Unit Tests for Graph Construction\\n```python\\ndef test_graph_construction():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Test that semantic graph construction works correctly.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    # Test 1: Simple case - 2 similar sentences\\n    sentences = [\\n        \\\"The cat sat on the mat.\\\",\\n        \\\"A cat was sitting on a mat.\\\"\\n    ]\\n    embeddings = model.encode(sentences)\\n    G = build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, threshold=0.3)\\n    \\n    assert G.number_of_nodes() == 2\\n    assert G.number_of_edges() >= 1  # Should have at least 1 edge (similar sentences)\\n    print('Test 1 PASSED: Similar sentences connected')\\n    \\n    # Test 2: Dissimilar sentences - should have no edges (or fewer edges)\\n    sentences = [\\n        \\\"The cat sat on the mat.\\\",\\n        \\\"Quantum entanglement violates local realism.\\\"\\n    ]\\n    embeddings = model.encode(sentences)\\n    G = build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, threshold=0.5)  # Higher threshold\\n    \\n    # With threshold=0.5, dissimilar sentences may not get an edge\\n    print(f'Test 2: Dissimilar sentences, edges={G.number_of_edges()} (expect 0 or 1)')\\n    \\n    # Test 3: Isolated sentences (single sentence)\\n    sentences = [\\\"Single sentence.\\\"]\\n    embeddings = model.encode(sentences)\\n    G = build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, threshold=0.3)\\n    \\n    assert G.number_of_nodes() == 1\\n    assert G.number_of_edges() == 0\\n    print('Test 3 PASSED: Single sentence handled')\\n    \\n    # Test 4: Empty text\\n    sentences = []\\n    G = build_semantic_graph(sentences, np.array([]), threshold=0.3)\\n    assert G.number_of_nodes() == 0\\n    print('Test 4 PASSED: Empty text handled')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T2: Test Resistance Computation\\n```python\\ndef test_resistance_computation():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Test Kirchhoff index computation on known graphs.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    # Test 1: Complete graph (all nodes connected)\\n    # For complete graph with n nodes and uniform edge weights:\\n    # Kirchhoff index = n - 1 (theoretical result)\\n    n = 5\\n    G_complete = nx.complete_graph(n)\\n    # Set edge weights to 1.0 (uniform resistance)\\n    for u, v in G_complete.edges():\\n        G_complete[u][v]['weight'] = 1.0\\n    \\n    kf = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G_complete)\\n    expected = n - 1  # Approximate for complete graph with unit weights\\n    print(f'Complete graph (n={n}): Kf={kf:.2f}, expected~{expected}')\\n    \\n    # Test 2: Path graph (sequential chain)\\n    G_path = nx.path_graph(5)  # 0-1-2-3-4\\n    for u, v in G_path.edges():\\n        G_path[u][v]['weight'] = 1.0\\n    \\n    kf_path = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G_path)\\n    print(f'Path graph (n=5): Kf={kf_path:.2f}')\\n    # Path graph should have higher resistance than complete graph\\n    assert kf_path > kf, 'Path graph should have higher resistance than complete graph'\\n    print('Test PASSED: Path graph has higher resistance than complete graph')\\n    \\n    # Test 3: Isolated nodes (disconnected graph)\\n    G_disconnected = nx.Graph()\\n    G_disconnected.add_nodes_from([0, 1, 2])\\n    G_disconnected.add_edge(0, 1, weight=1.0)\\n    # Node 2 is isolated\\n    \\n    try:\\n        kf_disc = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G_disconnected)\\n        print(f'Disconnected graph: Kf={kf_disc}')\\n        print('Warning: Disconnected graph should be handled specially')\\n    except Exception as e:\\n        print(f'Disconnected graph error (expected): {e}')\\n Add small epsilon to avoid log(0)\\n    else:\\n        return kf\\n```\\n\\n## PHASE 4: COMPREHENSIVE EVALUATION\\n\\n### Step 4.1: Main Evaluation on CLEAR Corpus\\n```python\\nimport pandas as pd\\nimport numpy as np\\nfrom scipy import stats\\nfrom sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error\\nimport json\\n\\ndef evaluate_readability_metric(texts, human_scores, output_prefix=''):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Comprehensive evaluation of the resistance metric.\\n    \\n    Args:\\n        texts: List of text passages\\n        human_scores: List of human readability ratings (1-100 scale)\\n        output_prefix: Prefix for output files\\n    \\n    Returns:\\n        results: Dict with all evaluation metrics\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    results = {}\\n    \\n    # Multiple normalization schemes\\n                resistance_scores['raw'].append(kf)\\n                resistance_scores['by_n'].append(kf / n_sent)\\n                resistance_scores['by_n_squared'].append(kf / (n_sent ** 2))\\n                resistance_scores['by_n_cubed'].append(kf / (n_sent ** 2 * (n_sent - 1)) if n_sent > 1 else kf)\\n            else:\\n                print(f'  Skipping text {idx}: {n_sent} sentences (need >= 2)')\\n        except Exception as e:\\n            print(f'  Error processing text {idx}: {e}')\\n    \\n    # CRITERION 1: Number of distinct values (>10 required)\\n        n_distinct = len(set(scores))\\n        print(f'\\\\n--- Scheme: {scheme} ---')\\n        print(f'  Distinct values: {n_distinct}')\\n        \\n        if n_distinct <= 10:\\n            print(f'  WARNING: Only {n_distinct} distinct values! Metric may be trivial.')\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 2: Pearson correlation with human judgments\\n        pearson_r, pearson_p = stats.pearsonr(scores, valid_human_scores)\\n        print(f'  Pearson r: {pearson_r:.4f} (p={pearson_p:.4f})')\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 3: Spearman correlation (rank-based)\\n        spearman_r, spearman_p = stats.spearmanr(scores, valid_human_scores)\\n        print(f'  Spearman r: {spearman_r:.4f} (p={spearman_p:.4f})')\\n        \\n        # CRITERION 4: MAE/RMSE on original 1-100 scale\\n        # First, fit a simple linear regression to map  reg = LinearRegression().fit(X, y)\\n        y_pred = reg.predict(X)\\n        \\n        mae = mean_absolute_error(y, y_pred)\\n        rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y, y_pred))\\n for n_sentences\\n        # Method: residualize both variables on n_sentences, then correlate residuals\\n        X_control = n_sent_array.reshape(-1, 1)\\n        \\n        # Residualize resistance scores\\n        reg_resist = LinearRegression().fit(X_control, scores)\\n        resid_resist = scores - reg_resist.predict(X_control)\\n        \\n        # Residualize human scores\\n        reg_human = LinearRegression().fit(X_control, valid_human_scores)\\n        resid_human = valid_human_scores - reg_human.predict(X_control)\\n        \\n        partial_r, partial_p = stats.pearsonr(resid_resist, resid_human)\\n        print(f'  Partial r (controlling for sentence count): {partial_r:.4f} (p={partial_p:.4f})')\\n        \\n        # Store results\\n sentence)\\n    sentences = [\\\"Single sentence.\\\"]\\n    embeddings = model.encode(sentences)\\n    G = build_semantic_graph(sentences, embeddings, threshold=0.3)\\n    \\n    assert build_semantic_graph(sentences, np.array([]), threshold=0.3)\\n    assert G.number_of_nodes() == 0\\n    print('Test 4 PASSED: Empty text handled')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T2: Test Resistance Computation\\n```python\\ndef test_resistance_computation():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Test Kirchhoff index computation on known graphs.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    # Test 1: Complete graph (all nodes connected)\\n    # For complete graph with n nodes and uniform edge weights:\\n    # Kirchhoff index = n - 1 (theoretical result)\\n    n = 5\\n    G_complete = nx.complete_graph(n)\\n    # Set edge weights to 1.0 (uniform resistance)\\n    for u, v in G_complete.edges():\\n        G_complete[u][v]['weight'] = 1.0\\n    \\n    kf = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G_complete)\\n    expected = n - 1  # Approximate for complete graph with unit weights\\n    print(f'Complete graph (n={n}): Kf={kf:.2f}, expected~{expected}')\\n    \\n    # Test 2: Path graph (sequential chain)\\n    G_path = nx.path_graph(5)  # 0-1-2-3-4\\n    for u, v in G_path.edges():\\n        G_path[u][v]['weight'] = 1.0\\n    \\n    kf_path = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G_path)\\n    print(f'Path graph (n=5): Kf={kf_path:.2f}')\\n    # Path graph should have higher resistance than complete graph\\n    assert kf_path > kf, 'Path graph should have higher resistance than complete graph'\\n    print('Test PASSED: Path graph has higher resistance than complete graph')\\n    \\n    # Test 3: Isolated nodes (disconnected graph)\\n    G_disconnected = nx.Graph()\\n    G_disconnected.add_nodes_from([0, 1, 2])\\n    G_disconnected.add_edge(0, 1, weight=1.0)\\n    # Node 2 is isolated\\n    \\n    try:\\n        kf_disc = compute_kirchhoff_index_networkx(G_disconnected)\\n        print(f'Disconnected graph: Kf={kf_disc}')\\n        print('Warning: Disconnected graph should be handled specially')\\n    except Exception as e:\\n        print(f'Disconnected graph error (expected): {e}')\\n        print('Test PASSED: Disconnected graph detected')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T3: Test on Tiny Dataset (N=10)\\n```python\\ndef test_tiny_experiment():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Run experiment on 10 texts to verify pipeline works.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    # Create 10 synthetic texts with known readability\\n    test_texts = [\\n        # Easy texts (short sentences, simple words)\\n        \\\"The cat sat. The dog ran. They played.\\\",  # 3 short sentences\\n        \\\"I like apples. You like bananas. We all like fruit.\\\",  # 3 simple sentences\\n        # Medium texts\\n        \\\"The researcher conducted a comprehensive analysis of the data. Results indicated significant findings. The study was published in a journal.\\\",  # 3 medium sentences\\n        # Hard texts (long sentences, complex words)\\n        \\\"The epistemological underpinnings of contemporary hermeneutic phenomenology necessitate a nuanced understanding of ontological differentiation that transcends traditional metaphysical binaries.\\\",  # 1 very complex sentence\\n        \\\"Quantum entanglement, a phenomenon wherein particles become inextricably linked such that the state of one instantaneously influences the state of another regardless of spatial separation, challenges classical intuitions about locality and causality.\\\",  # 1 very long, complex sentence\\n    ] * 2  # Duplicate to get 10 texts\\n    \\n    # Synthetic readability scores (1-100)\\n    test_scores = [85, 80, 60, 30, 20, 85, 80, 60, 30, 20]\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TINY EXPERIMENT (N=10) ===')\\n    \\n    # Run evaluation\\n    results, valid_indices = evaluate_readability_metric(test_texts, test_scores)\\n    \\n    # Check results\\n    for scheme, res in results.items():\\n        print(f'\\\\nScheme: {scheme}')\\n        print(f'  Distinct values: {res[\\\"n_distinct_values\\\"]}')\\n        print(f'  Pearson r: {res[\\\"pearson_r\\\"]:.4f}')\\n        \\n        if res['n_distinct_values'] < 3:\\n            print('  WARNING: Too few distinct values even on tiny test!')\\n            print('  DIAGNOSTIC: Check graph construction and normalization')\\n    \\n    print('\\\\nTiny experiment complete. If results look reasonable, proceed to full experiment.')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T4: Validate CLEAR Corpus Loading\\n```python\\ndef test_clear_corpus_loading():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Test that CLEAR corpus can be downloaded and parsed.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TEST: CLEAR Corpus Loading ===')\\n    \\n    try:\\n        url = 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/scrosseye/CLEAR-Corpus/main/data/clear_corpus.csv'\\n        df = pd.read_csv(url)\\n        \\n        print(f'SUCCESS: Loaded {len(df)} rows')\\n        print(f'Columns: {df.columns.tolist()}')\\n        print(f'First 3 rows:')\\n        print(df.head(3))\\n        \\n        # Check for required columns\\n        text_col = None\\n        score_col = None\\n        \\n        for col in df.columns:\\n            if col.lower() in ['excerpt', 'text', 'passage']:\\n                text_col = col\\n            if 'readability' in col.lower() or 'score' in col.lower():\\n                score_col = col\\n        \\n        if text_col and score_col:\\n            print(f'SUCCESS: Found text column \\\"{text_col}\\\" and score column \\\"{score_col}\\\"')\\n            print(f'Score range: [{df[score_col].min()}, {df[score_col].max()}]')\\n            \\n            # Check a sample text\\n            sample_text = df[text_col].iloc[0]\\n            print(f'Sample text (first 100 chars): {sample_text[:100]}')\\n            \\n            return True\\n        else:\\n            print('WARNING: Could not identify text/score columns')\\n            print('Executor must manually inspect and specify column names')\\n            return False\\n            \\n    except Exception as e:\\n        print(f'FAILED: {e}')\\n        print('Will use fallback dataset (agentlans/readability)')\\n        return False\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T5: Runtime Test\\n```python\\ndef test_runtime_scaling():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Verify that runtime is acceptable for typical text lengths.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TEST: Runtime Scaling ===')\\n    \\n    test_cases = [\\n        ('Short (5 sentences)', 'This is a test. It has few sentences. Not very long. But it works. The end.'),\\n        ('Medium (10 sentences)', 'Sentence one. Sentence two. Sentence three. Sentence four. Sentence five. Sentence six. Sentence seven. Sentence eight. Sentence nine. Sentence ten.'),\\n        ('Long (20 sentences)', 'S1. ' * 20),  # Will be processed as 20 sentences\\n    ]\\n    \\n    for name, text in test_cases:\\n        start = time.time()\\n        kf, n, G = compute_resistance_metric(text, similarity_threshold=0.3)\\n        elapsed = time.time() - start\\n        \\n        print(f'{name}: n={n}, time={elapsed:.3f}s, Kf={kf:.2f}')\\n        \\n        if elapsed > 5.0:\\n            print(f'  WARNING: Slow! {elapsed:.1f}s for {n} sentences')\\n            print(f'  SUGGESTION: Use k-NN graph or increase similarity threshold')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T6: Validate Distinct Values Requirement\\n```python\\ndef test_distinct_values():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"CRITICAL TEST: Verify metric produces enough distinct values.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== TEST: Distinct Values (Reviewer Issue #1) ===')\\n    \\n    # Load a few diverse texts from CLEAR corpus (or use test texts)\\n    test_texts = [\\n        \\\"Short simple text. Few sentences.\\\",  # Very short\\n        \\\"This is a medium length text. It has a few more sentences. Each sentence is moderately complex. The ideas are somewhat connected.\\\",  # Medium\\n        \\\"The epistemological ramifications of quantum decoherence necessitate a paradigmatic reconceptualization of macroscopic realism that transcends classical metaphysical frameworks.\\\" * 5,  # Very complex, repeated\\n        \\\"Alice went to the store. Bob went to the park. They met at the cafe. The cafe was closed. They went home.\\\" * 3,  # Narratives\\n    ]\\n    \\n    for scheme in ['raw', 'by_n', 'by_n_squared']:\\n        scores = []\\n        for text in test_texts:\\n            kf, n, G = compute_resistance_metric(text, threshold=0.3)\\n            if kf:\\n                normalized = normalize_kirchhoff_index(kf, n, scheme)\\n                scores.append(normalized)\\n        \\n        n_distinct = len(set([round(s, 4) for s in scores]))\\n        print(f'Scheme \\\"{scheme}\\\": {n_distinct} distinct values from {len(scores)} texts')\\n        \\n        if n_distinct <= 2:\\n            print('  CRITICAL FAILURE: Metric produces too few distinct values!')\\n            print('  This was the problem with the first experiment.')\\n            print('  DEBUG: Check graph construction - are all graphs identical?')\\n            print('  DEBUG: Check normalization - is division by n^2 collapsing values?')\\n```\\n\\n### Phase T7: Integration Test (Full Pipeline on 50 Texts)\\n```python\\ndef integration_test():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Run full pipeline on 50 texts to catch integration issues.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    \\n    print('\\\\n=== INTEGRATION TEST (50 texts) ===')\\n    \\n    # Try to load CLEAR corpus\\n    if test_clear_corpus_loading():\\n        # Use actual CLEAR data\\n        df = pd.read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/scrosseye/CLEAR-Corpus/main/data/clear_corpus.csv')\\n        texts = df['excerpt'].tolist()[:50]\\n        scores = df['readability_score'].tolist()[:50]\\n    else:\\n        # Use synthetic test data\\n        texts = [f'Test text number {i}. With some sentences. And ideas.' for i in range(50)]\\n        scores = list(range(50))  # Synthetic scores 0-49\\n    \\n    # Run full evaluation\\n    results, valid_idx = evaluate_readability_metric(texts, scores)\\n    \\n    # Check all reviewer issues\\n    print('\\\\n--- REVIEWER ISSUES CHECKLIST ---')\\n    \\n    for scheme, res in results.items():\\n        print(f'\\\\nScheme: {scheme}')\\n        \\n        # Issue 1: Continuous values\\n        print(f'  [1] Distinct values: {res[\\\"n_distinct_values\\\"]} (need >10: {\\\"PASS\\\" if res[\\\"n_distinct_values\\\"] > 10 else \\\"FAIL\\\"})')\\n        \\n        # Issue 2: Partial correlation\\n        print(f'  [2] Partial r (control sent count): {res[\\\"partial_r\\\"]:.4f} (need >0: {\\\"PASS\\\" if res[\\\"partial_r\\\"] > 0 else \\\"FAIL\\\"})')\\n        \\n        # Issue 3: Semantic edges\\n        print(f'  [3] Using semantic edges: True (by design)')\\n        \\n        # Issue 4: Established benchmark\\n        print(f'  [4] Using CLEAR corpus: True')\\n    \\n    print('\\\\nIntegration test complete. Review results before running full experiment.')\\n```\\n\\n### Test Execution Order\\n```\\n1. test_graph_construction()          # Unit test: graph building works\\n2. test_resistance_computation()     # Unit test: Kf computation works  \\n3. test_clear_corpus_loading()       # Integration: can load evaluation data\\n4. test_distinct_values()            # CRITICAL: metric isnt trivial\\n5. test_tiny_experiment()            # Integration: full pipeline on N=10\\n6. test_runtime_scaling()            # Performance: runtime acceptable?\\n7. integration_test()                # Full: 50 texts, all metrics\\n\\nOnly proceed to full experiment if ALL tests pass.\\n```",
